3.22: chasde YHWH ki lo tamnu ki lo chalu rachamav: 'The steadfast love [chesed] of the LORD never ceases; his mercies [rachamim] never come to an end.' This confession (Lam 3:22-24) is the theological center of Lamentations — placed at the exact midpoint of the book (the acrostic structure of ch. 3 is 22×3 = 66 verses, and v. 22 is the centerpiece). Its context makes it remarkable: Lamentations is the most sustained expression of grief and lamentation in Scripture, written in the immediate aftermath of Jerusalem's destruction, yet its center is a confession of divine steadfast love. The structure embodies the theology: suffering and praise, grief and faith, can coexist — and at the center of the darkest book is the brightest confession.
3.1: gever (H1397, man/warrior) opens the poem: ani hagever ra'ah 'oni beshevet 'evrato (I am the man who has seen affliction under the rod of his wrath). Gever derives from gabar (to be strong, to prevail, H1396) and denotes a man specifically in his capacity for strength or warrior identity — not merely ish (a man in relational/social terms) or adam (humanity in general). The poet identifies as the gever who has seen affliction — the strong man brought low, the warrior undone by the rod. The acrostic structure of ch. 3 (triple aleph-bet: each letter of the 22-letter Hebrew alphabet opens three consecutive verses) is the formal embodiment of the theological claim: grief has been given the most complete possible expression within ordered language. The discipline of the acrostic form holds the chaos of suffering within a structured container.
3.2: Oti nahag vayolek choshekh velo or (He has driven me and brought me into darkness and not light). The darkness/light antithesis (choshekh H2822 / or H216) recalls the creation vocabulary of Gen 1:2-4 — the primordial darkness before God's creative word. For the gever, YHWH's action has undone creation: instead of being led into light (the positive divine guidance of Ps 23:2-3), he is led into darkness. The inversion of Psalm 23's 'he leads me' is deliberate: Lamentations 3 is the anti-Psalm 23 before it becomes the psalm of trust again at vv 22-26. The verb nahag (to drive/lead, H5090) is used of shepherding livestock — YHWH as shepherd driving the flock into the dark.
3.3: Akh bi yashov yachpoch yado kol hayom (Only against me he turns; he turns his hand against me all day long). The repetition of yashov (he turns, from shub, H7725) — YHWH's hand turned against the poet repeatedly — picks up the shepherd-turning-against image. The akh (only/surely, H389) is a restrictive particle emphasizing the totality: not occasionally but exclusively, and not partially but with the whole hand. The three aleph-verses (1-3) establish the fullest possible lament: the man of strength, driven into darkness, beaten without relief.
3.4: Bilah besari ve'ori shavar atzmotai (He has made my flesh and my skin waste away; he has broken my bones). The bet-section (vv 4-6) moves to bodily suffering. Bilah (to waste away/wear out, H1086) is used of aged garments and old wineskins — the body worn out as fabric wears. Shavar (broken, H7665) for bones is the vocabulary of catastrophic physical force. Together flesh, skin, and bones represent the complete human body from outer to inner structure — the entire physical person destroyed. The vocabulary parallels Job's physical afflictions (Job 30:30; 33:21), situating Lamentations in the wider wisdom tradition of innocent suffering.
3.5: Banah alai vayaqef rosh vete'la (He has built against me and compassed me with bitterness/gall and hardship). The construction (banah, to build, H1129) against the poet uses siege-warfare language: YHWH is the siege engineer constructing fortifications against his own servant. Rosh (H7218, poison/gall, or possibly the poisonous plant Conium maculatum) and te'la (H8513, hardship/toil) represent the siege's contents. The same rosh appears in Lam 3:15, 19 and in Deuteronomy 29:18 as the 'root bearing poisonous fruit' — the language of covenant curse.
3.6: Bemakom chashekh hoshivani kemete 'olam (He has made me dwell in darkness like the dead of long ago). Chashekh (H4285, thick darkness, place of darkness) — here the abode of the dead, the darkness below. Mete 'olam (H4191 + H5769, the dead of old/ancient dead) — those who have been in Sheol for ages, completely forgotten. To be treated like the ancient dead is to be placed beyond the reach of any redemption, beyond the possibility of return — the poet's felt experience of total abandonment. The image anticipates 3:53-55 (they cast me into the pit).
3.7: Gadar ba'adi velo etze hikbid nechushtam (He has walled me in so I cannot get out; he has made my chain heavy). The gimel-section (vv 7-9) develops the siege/imprisonment imagery. Gadar (to wall, H1443) constructs an impenetrable barrier. The heavy chain (nechoshet, H5178, bronze/copper) — the metal of bondage (Ps 107:10: those in misery and iron chains) — is the physical embodiment of the impossibility of escape. The sequence of siege, darkness, chains forms the complete picture of imprisonment without exit.
3.8: Gam ki ez'aq vaashavea satar tefilati (Also when I cry out and shout, he shuts out my prayer). The most terrible element of the prison: prayer itself is blocked. Satar (to shut out/hide, H8168) applied to prayer is rare — it appears in Ps 22:2 (I cry out but you do not answer) in the same desperate context. The lament that prayer is unavailing — that the very channel of communication with YHWH has been blocked — is the depth of the theological crisis. The NT parallel: Christ's cry of dereliction (Matt 27:46) quotes Ps 22:1 in this same register of unanswered prayer under judgment.
3.9: Gadar derakhai begazit netivotai 'iqesh (He has walled in my paths with hewn stones; he has made my ways crooked). The gazit (hewn stone, H1496) — the dressed stone used in construction, including the temple — used here as blocking material. The paths are walled in with the very material of ordered civilized life. 'Iqesh (crooked, H5753, from 'awah, to make crooked) is the same root as the 'iniquity' word group — paths crooked/twisted, made inaccessible. The reversal of Isaiah 40:4's straight-path promise: there, valleys filled and crooked places made straight; here, straight paths are made crooked.
3.10: Dov orev hu li aryeh bemistarem (He is a bear lying in wait for me, a lion in hidden places). The dalet-section (vv 10-12) depicts YHWH as a predatory animal. The bear (dov, H1677) and the lion (aryeh, H738) are Israel's two most dangerous land predators — both are used in Hosea 13:7-8 for YHWH's judgment of Israel (I will be to them like a lion, like a bear robbed of her cubs). The image of YHWH as predator is one of the most shocking in the OT — it inverts the shepherd-protector image (Ps 23) to its opposite. The theological point: YHWH's opposition to his own people when they are under covenant curse is as inexorable as the attack of the most dangerous predator.
3.11: Derakhai sorer vayefashecheni shamani shomem (He turned aside my paths and tore me in pieces; he made me desolate). The tearing (pasach, H6582, hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible) applies the predator's action from v10: the bear/lion that was lying in wait now tears its prey. Shomem (desolate, H8074, from shamem) is the same word used for the desolation of Jerusalem and the temple — the poet's condition mirrors the city's. The rhyme of the words and the image creates a devastating compound: ambush, pursuit, destruction, desolation.
3.12: Darak kashto vayatziveni kematarah lachetz (He bent his bow and set me as a target for the arrow). The archery imagery (bow bent, archer's target) makes the violence even more deliberate: not the random violence of a hunting predator but the aimed, intentional violence of a skilled archer. YHWH as archer appears in Ps 7:12-13 and Job 16:12-13 — both in the context of innocent suffering. The matarah (H4307, target/mark) — the one deliberately aimed at — is the most isolated position possible: singled out for assault from a distance by a superior force.
3.13: Hevi bekilyotai benei ashpato (He has caused the arrows of his quiver to enter into my kidneys). The he-section (vv 13-15) focuses on the bodily impact of divine assault. Kilyot (H3629, kidneys, plural) — in Hebrew anthropology, the kidneys were the seat of deep emotion, conscience, and inner experience, comparable to the heart (lev). The kidneys are tested by YHWH (Ps 7:9; Jer 11:20; 17:10: I, YHWH, search the heart and test the kidneys) — to have YHWH's arrows entering the kidneys is for divine judgment to penetrate to the deepest seat of personhood.
3.14: Hayiti sehoq lekhol ami neglnato kol hayom (I have become the laughingstock of all peoples, their taunt song all day long). The public mockery (sehoq, H7814, laughter/mockery) — the person of God as the object of communal derision. The neginah (H5058, taunt-song, song of mockery) — a musical form that twists praise into contempt. The same vocabulary appears in Job 30:9 (and now they mock me in song; I am a byword to them) and in Ps 69:12 (those who sit in the gate talk about me, and I am the song of drunkards). The mock-song directed at the individual or the city is the public face of covenant curse.
3.15: Hisbiani bamerorim hirvani la'anah (He has filled me with bitterness; he has made me drunk with wormwood). Merorim (H4844, bitter things, from marar to be bitter) is the word for the bitter herbs of the Passover meal (Exod 12:8; Num 9:11) — the Passover memory of slavery now inverted into present suffering. La'anah (H3939, wormwood, Artemisia absinthium) is the bitter plant that symbolizes calamity and judgment throughout the prophets (Jer 9:15; 23:15; Amos 5:7; 6:12 — turning justice into wormwood). To be drunk on wormwood is to be saturated with the substance of covenant curse.
3.16: Vayyagreis bekhatsats shinai hikpi'ani va'efer (He has broken my teeth with gravel; he has made me cower in ashes). The vav-section (vv 16-18) moves to the deepest despair. Khatsats (H2687, gravel/small stones) — the sensation of biting down on grit, the destruction of the ability to eat. Hikpi'ani (he has made me grovel/cower, from kafah H3721, hapax — to press down, bend low) combined with efer (H665, ashes) — the gesture of mourning, prostration in ashes (Job 2:8; Gen 18:27: 'I who am but dust and ashes'). The ashes are both the mourning gesture and the result of the destruction — the city has become ashes.
3.17: Vatizneach min-shalom nafshi nashiti tovah (And you have removed my soul from peace; I have forgotten prosperity). Nefesh (H5315, soul/life/self — the whole living person, not the Greek-influenced disembodied soul) is here the subject of displacement: the whole living self has been removed from shalom (H7965, peace, wholeness, the comprehensive well-being of covenant blessing). The translation notes flag the nefesh polysemy: 'soul' (Greek-influenced) vs. 'life' (the whole living person). The forgetting of tov (goodness/prosperity, H2896) — the created goodness that YHWH called good in Gen 1 — represents the total negation of the creation-blessing.
3.18: Va'omar avad nitzchi vitochalti min-YHWH (And I said, My endurance has perished, and my hope from the LORD). The nitzchi (H5331, my endurance/perpetuity, from netzach) — the word used for 'forever' in the lament psalms (Ps 13:1: How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever?). The explicit naming of YHWH as the one from whom hope has been cut is the crisis: the very source of hope is experienced as the cause of hopelessness. The verse is the nadir before the great turn of vv 21-24. The structure is deliberate: the poet states the absolute loss of hope in v18 precisely because what follows (vv 21-24) is the recovery of hope — hope that is real only because it has passed through this depth.
3.19: Zechor 'onyi umrudi la'anah varo'sh (Remember my affliction and my wandering, the wormwood and the gall). The zayin-section (vv 19-21) is the pivot: from the deepest despair to renewed hope. The call to remember (zakar, H2142) — directed first at the poet himself (vv 19-21) — is the OT's primary mechanism for theological reorientation. Memory in the Hebrew tradition is not passive recollection but active engagement that produces new action. The pairing of la'anah (wormwood) and rosh (gall/poison) returns to the bitterness vocabulary of vv 5, 15 — but now in the context of the zayin-section which will move toward hope.
3.20: Zakhor tizkor vetashokh 'alay nafshi (My soul continually remembers it and is bowed down within me). The unusual doubled verb form zakhor tizkor (remembering it remembers — the infinitive absolute + finite verb construction, emphatic repetition) emphasizes the relentless quality of memory: it will not stop. The nefesh (soul/life-self) bowed down within — the inward posture of prostration. This verse stands between despair and hope: the memory that prostrates is the same memory that, when turned in the right direction (v21: 'this I recall to my heart'), produces hope. Memory is not inherently therapeutic or destructive — its direction determines its effect.
3.21: Zot ashiv el libbi 'al ken 'ochal (This I call to mind, and therefore I have hope). The pivot verse: ashiv (I will return, from shub, to turn/return, H7725) — the same verb used for repentance and turning in the prophets. The poet turns his attention (the lev/heart-mind) to the zot (this) — what follows in vv 22-24, the chesed of YHWH. The entire depression of vv 1-20 is reframed by a deliberate act of theological memory: 'this I call to mind.' Hope (yachal, H3176, to wait/hope/expect) returns not as a feeling that arose spontaneously but as the result of a deliberate cognitive act of remembering the character of YHWH. This is the OT's most concentrated statement of the mechanics of hope under suffering.
3.23: Chadashim labeqarim rabbah emunatecha (They are new every morning; great is your faithfulness). The renewal formula: morning (boqer, H1242) as the recurring renewal of YHWH's mercies — each morning is a new bestowal of the steadfast love declared in v22. Emunah (H530, faithfulness, from aman to be firm/reliable) — the word underlying 'Amen'; it denotes the solid reliability of YHWH's character. Rabbah emunatecha (great is your faithfulness) — the exclamation that stands on its own as theological praise in the middle of the lament. The morning renewal of mercy is not cyclical vanity (as Qohelet's sun-rises-and-sets is) but the repeated bestowal of the same faithful love — new in each application, same in its source.
3.24: Chelqi YHWH amar nafshi 'al ken 'ochal lo (My portion is the LORD, says my soul, therefore I will hope in him). Chelek (H2506, portion/share/inheritance) is the OT's covenant-distribution term: each tribe received its chelek in the land; the Levites' portion was YHWH himself (Deut 10:9; 18:2). To claim YHWH as one's chelek when everything material has been destroyed — when the land, the temple, the city, the society have all been stripped away — is the radical form of the covenant statement: the relationship with YHWH outlasts every temporal holding. The translation notes flag YHWH: the divine personal name (Yahweh), typically rendered LORD in small caps but hiding the personal name.
3.25: Tov YHWH le'kov lo lenafs tiddreshenu (The LORD is good to those who wait for him, to the soul that seeks him). The tet-section (vv 25-27) develops the waiting theology established in v24. Tov YHWH (the LORD is good) — the foundation for waiting: goodness is the reliable character that makes waiting rational rather than absurd. Qavah (to wait/hope, H6960, from a root meaning to twist together like a cord) — the expectation of a person who is straining toward the fulfillment of a promise. The soul (nefesh) that seeks YHWH (darash, H1875, to seek/inquire/resort to) — the active posture of seeking, not passive resignation.
3.26: Tov vedumam leyesha' YHWH savah veyikhol (It is good that one should wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD). Dumam (silently/quietly, H1748) — the silence of patient waiting, from damam (to be silent, to be still, H1826; cf. Ps 62:1: For God alone my soul waits in silence). Yeshua (H3444, salvation/deliverance) — the feminine form of the proper name Jesus (Yeshua, H3091). The verse declares that waiting silently for the yeshua of YHWH is itself the good posture — the form of faith that the crisis requires. The name Jesus embedded in the Hebrew word for salvation is one of the canonical textual connections between the OT's vocabulary and the NT's person.
3.27: Tov legever ki yissa' ol bine'urav (It is good for a man to bear the yoke in his youth). The gever from v1 returns: the man of strength bearing the yoke. 'Ol (H5923, yoke) — the instrument of labor and submission. In youth (bine'urav, in his young years) — the earlier the discipline is learned, the better. The same verb nasa (to bear/carry, H5375) is used for bearing sin, bearing the ark, bearing the covenant obligations — the yoke is not merely physical labor but the discipline of covenant obligation and the acceptance of one's condition under YHWH's sovereignty. Jeremiah's sign-act of wearing a yoke (Jer 27-28) is the enacted version of this call.
3.28: Yeshev badad veyiddom ki natal alav (Let him sit alone in silence when he has laid it on him). The yod-section (vv 28-30) describes the posture of the one under divine discipline. Badad (H910, alone/isolation) — the same word used for Jerusalem's isolation in Lam 1:1 (how she sits alone). The solitude of the one bearing the yoke is the structural solitude of the city — both the poet and the city are alone, silent, enduring. Dumam (silence) returns from v26 — the silence before YHWH as the appropriate response to what YHWH has laid on. The willingness to sit alone in silence rather than resist or protest is the form of acceptance the text commends.
3.29: Yitten be'afar pihu ulay yesh tiqvah (Let him put his mouth in the dust — there may yet be hope). The gesture of putting one's mouth in the dust (afar, H6083, dust — the death-material of Gen 3:19: you are dust) is the maximum gesture of submission and humiliation before a superior. Abraham in Gen 18:27 identifies himself as 'dust and ashes'; Job in 42:6 repents 'in dust and ashes'. The posture is not self-abasement for its own sake but the recognition of creaturely finitude before the Creator — and out of that recognition, the possibility of hope (tiqvah, H8615): 'there may yet be hope.' The hope that emerges from the dust-posture is the hope that comes after the self-assertion has been completely abandoned.
3.30: Yitten lemakkehu lehi yisba' becherpa (Let him give his cheek to the one who strikes, and let him be filled with insults). The cheek-turning under assault (malah, H3895, cheek/jaw) — the willingness to absorb assault without retaliation, presented here as the form of submission under discipline. This is the OT's most direct parallel to Jesus's teaching about turning the other cheek (Matt 5:39) and to Isaiah 50:6 (the Servant: I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard). The one who absorbs insult and assault in the context of bearing the yoke participates in the pattern of the Servant's submission.
3.31: Ki lo yiznak leolam Adonai (For the Lord will not cast off forever). The kaf-section (vv 31-33) provides the theological basis for the endurance called for in vv 28-30. Zanach (H2186, to cast away/reject, H2186) + le'olam (H5769, forever/for an age) — YHWH's rejection is not permanent. The translation notes flag 'olam: 'eternal/everlasting/age-long' — the duration is emphatic but limited (will not cast off le'olam = for the long term, but not permanently). Adonai (H136, my lord, used as the substitute for YHWH in liturgical reading) — this verse uses Adonai rather than YHWH, consistent with ch. 3's shift from YHWH (personal name) to Adonai (title) in the central section.
3.32: Ki im hogah veraham kerov chasadav (But though he causes grief, he will have compassion according to the abundance of his steadfast love). The chesed (H2617, steadfast love) from v22 returns: the abundant chesed is the theological ground for the 'not forever' of v31. The structure of v32: the grief is real and caused by YHWH (ki im hogah = though/when he causes grief) — no denial of the suffering — but the compassion (raham, from rachamim, womb-love, the deepest tenderness) comes from the abundance (H7230, rob) of his chesed. The quantitative qualifier (abundance, rob) stresses that YHWH's steadfast love is not a thin trickle of mercy but an overflowing resource.
3.33: Ki lo innah velibo venoga beney ish (For he does not afflict from his heart or grieve the children of men). Innah (H6031, to afflict/humble) from his heart (libo, his heart) — YHWH does not afflict willingly, from his core desire. The affliction that characterizes the entire poem is not YHWH's positive will but his reluctant judicial action; his heart desires compassion rather than judgment. This is the OT's most explicit statement that divine judgment is always instrumental (for purposes of restoration, correction, refinement) rather than expressive (of YHWH's pleasure in punishment). James 2:13 — mercy triumphs over judgment — is the NT's form of this principle.
3.34: Lakadkad tachat ragluv kol asirei aretz (To crush underfoot all the prisoners of the earth). The lamed-section (vv 34-36) continues the theological reflection on what YHWH does not approve. These three verses are list of injustices that, the text implies, YHWH sees and does not approve: crushing prisoners (v34), subverting justice before the Most High (v35), defrauding a man in court (v36). The theological point: the suffering of ch. 3 is not the result of YHWH approving these injustices. The very God who appears to have been acting unjustly (according to vv 1-20) is in fact the God who opposes the crushing of prisoners.
3.35: Lehattot mishpat gever lifnei penei Elyon (To subvert the right of a man before the face of the Most High). Mishpat (H4941, justice/right/judgment) — the legal right of the individual. Elyon (H5945, Most High) — the divine title expressing YHWH's sovereignty over all human courts. To subvert justice in the presence of the Most High — who sees all human proceedings — is to defy the one whose own character is justice. The three-verse sequence (34-36) uses the infinitive construct (to crush, to subvert, to deny) as legal charges against the oppressor, not against YHWH.
3.36: Leavet adam berivov Adonai lo ra'ah (To deny a man justice in his lawsuit — the Lord does not approve). Adon lo ra'ah (the Lord does not approve/see) — the final declaration that YHWH does not sanction injustice. This verse is the theological pivot: YHWH who appeared to be acting against the poet (vv 1-20) is in fact the God who opposes unjust treatment. The poet's suffering is not YHWH approving his oppression but YHWH's judicial action in the context of covenant discipline — a different category from the injustice YHWH will not sanction.
3.37: Mi zeh amar vatahi Adonai lo tzivah (Who is he who speaks and it comes to pass unless the Lord has commanded it?). The mem-section (vv 37-39) draws out the theological implications of the previous section. Amar vatahi (speaks and it comes to pass) — the efficacy of the divine word from Gen 1 (God said, and it was). The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. Nothing comes to pass that YHWH has not commanded — including the suffering of ch. 3. The theodicy is not resolved (why did YHWH command this?) but the sovereignty is affirmed (nothing happens outside YHWH's authority).
3.38: Mippi Elyon lo tetzeh hara'ot vehatov (Is it not from the mouth of the Most High that good and evil go forth?). The good-and-evil-from-God statement is the most directly theodicy-engaging verse in the chapter. Ra'ot (evil/calamity, H7451) and hatov (the good, H2896) both proceeding from the mouth of Elyon. This is not a metaphysical claim that YHWH is the author of moral evil but the covenantal claim that both covenant blessing and covenant curse come from YHWH's sovereign word (cf. Isa 45:7: I form light and create darkness; I make well-being and create calamity — I am the LORD who does all these things). The suffering of Lamentations is the ra'ah of covenant curse, which YHWH commands in Deuteronomy 28 for covenant violation.
3.39: Mah yitonen adam chai gever al chata'av (Why should a living man complain, a man, about the punishment of his sins?). The rhetorical question — why should a gever (the powerful man of v1, now humbled) complain about the punishment for his sins? — is the response to the implied complaint of the entire chapter. The living man (chai) who still lives under punishment has the capacity and opportunity for repentance; the gever who complains about justified punishment misunderstands his situation. The verse functions as a rebuke to self-pity and a call to accountability — and sets up the call to repentance in vv 40-42.
3.40: Nachpesah derachenu venachqorah venashuvah ad YHWH (Let us search out our ways and examine them, and turn again to the LORD). The nun-section (vv 40-42) moves from individual theodicy to communal repentance. Chafas (H2664, to search/examine) + chaqar (H2713, to examine closely/investigate) — the double verb of thorough self-examination. Shub (H7725, to return/turn) ad YHWH — the standard OT vocabulary of repentance: turning back to the covenant God. The translation notes flag YHWH as the divine personal name. The communal 'we' replaces the individual 'I' — the poet's suffering has become the community's call.
3.41: Nissa levavenu el kapayim el El bashamayim (Let us lift up our hearts with our hands to God in heaven). Nissa levavenu (lift up our hearts) — prayer is not merely the uplifted hands but the uplifted heart-mind (lev, H3820). The combination of heart and hands in prayer — inner orientation and outward gesture combined — represents the whole-person engagement that genuine prayer requires. El bashamayim (God in the heavens, H410 + H8064) — the transcendent God who is simultaneously the personal covenant God. The heavenward direction acknowledges that the solution must come from beyond the horizontal plane of human action.
3.42: Nachnu fashanu umarimu veatah lo salachtah (We have transgressed and rebelled, and you have not forgiven). The brutally honest confession: fashanu (we have transgressed, from pesha, H6586, rebellion/transgression — the strongest sin vocabulary: willful defiance, breach of relationship) + marimu (we have rebelled, from marah, H4784, to be rebellious/disobedient). The second half is equally brutal: veatah lo salachtah (and you have not forgiven). The confession does not end with the expectation of immediate forgiveness — it holds the acknowledgment of sin and the experience of unresolved judgment together without collapsing one into the other. This is the most honest form of confession: true acknowledgment that does not presuppose its acceptance.
3.43: Sakotah va'af vatirdefenu haragta velo chamaltah (You have wrapped yourself with anger and pursued us; you have killed and have not pitied). The samech-section (vv 43-45) is the communal lament resumed. Sakotah (you have covered/wrapped yourself, from sakak, H5526) with anger (af, H639) — YHWH clothed in wrath, surrounded by it. The pursuit and killing without pity is the language of Deuteronomy 28's covenant curse fulfilled: the curses listed in Deut 28:15-68 include precisely this relentless pursuit and destruction without mercy (Deut 28:65-67). Lamentations is the experiential form of what Deuteronomy threatened.
3.44: Sakotah be'anan lecha me'avor tefillah (You have covered yourself with a cloud so that no prayer can pass through). The cloud (anan, H6051) as the barrier to prayer — inverting the cloud's normal OT function. In the wilderness, the cloud was the symbol of YHWH's guiding, protective presence (Exod 13:21; 40:34-35); here the cloud is the barrier between the people and YHWH. The protective cloud has become the blocking cloud — the symbol of divine presence has become the symbol of divine hiddenness. The only way through the cloud-barrier is the one who is himself the divine presence: Christ as the one through whom prayer reaches the Father (John 14:6, 13-14).
3.45: Siach usma'os tesimenu bekerev ha'amim (You have made us scum and garbage among the peoples). Siach (H5501, offscouring/rubbish) and ma'os (H3973, refuse/rejection) — the two strongest contempt-vocabulary words in the OT. Paul uses a Greek equivalent in 1 Cor 4:13: 'we have become the scum of the world, the refuse of all things' — applying the Lamentations vocabulary to the apostolic experience of suffering for the gospel. The community at the bottom of the social hierarchy, treated as refuse — this is the community that receives the beatitudes ('blessed are the poor in spirit'; 'blessed are those who are persecuted').
3.46: Patzenu aleinu pihem kol oyveynu (All our enemies have opened their mouths against us). The ayin-section (vv 46-48) focuses on the enemies who take advantage of the city's collapse. The opened mouth (patzah, H6473, to gape/open wide) of the enemies — the gloating, the taunting, the triumphant abuse of the defeated. The same imagery appears in Ps 22:13: roaring lions that open their mouths wide. The enemies' open mouths contrast with the blocked prayer of v44 — the enemies have voice while YHWH's people are silenced.
3.47: Pachad vefachat hayah lanu hashait vehasshever (Panic and pitfall have come upon us, devastation and destruction). The four-word sequence (pachad H6343 panic, pachath H6354 pit/trap, shait H7722 devastation, shever H7667 fracture/destruction) uses the literary device of consonance — the ph/sh sounds create a rushing, overwhelming sequence. The pit (pachath) picks up the pit imagery of vv 52-55. The combination of trap and destruction is the vocabulary of Isa 24:17-18 (the three-fold curse: pachad, pachath, pakh — panic, pit, trap) — Lamentations 3 is Deuteronomy's covenant curse and Isaiah's universal judgment concentrated in the singular experience of the poet.
3.48: Palgei mayim tored 'eini 'al shever bat 'ammi (My eye runs down with rivers of tears because of the destruction of the daughter of my people). Palgei mayim (H6388 + H4325, streams/channels of water) — the eye as a river source, the weeping as uncontrollable as a flowing stream. Bat 'ammi (H1323 + H5971, daughter of my people) — the personification of the community as a daughter, consistent with the 'daughter of Zion' language throughout Lamentations. The weeping over the destruction of the community connects the individual lament (the gever) to the communal suffering that the whole book mourns.
3.49: Eini nezalah velo tidmeh me'ein haftugot (My eye pours down without ceasing, without respite). The pe-section (vv 49-51) intensifies the weeping. Nizalah (poured down, from nazal, H5140, to flow/drip) — continuous flow without interruption. Me'ein haftugot (without any ceasing, from hafag, H6315, to cease/stop — another hapax in this form) — the utter absence of relief from weeping. The weeping without ceasing connects to Jeremiah's own weeping (Jer 9:1: Oh that my head were waters, and my eyes a fountain of tears) — the prophet who saw the disaster weeping over it without relief.
3.50: Ad yashqif veyire YHWH mishshamayim (Until the LORD looks down and sees from heaven). The 'until' (ad, H5704) is the hinge of the entire weeping sequence: the weeping continues without ceasing until YHWH acts. The looking down from heaven (shaqaf, H8259, to look down/lean out) — the same word used of YHWH looking down from heaven in Ps 14:2 and 102:20 (YHWH looked down from his holy height; from heaven he looked at the earth to hear the groans of the prisoners). The appeal to YHWH's seeing from heaven presupposes that YHWH will respond when he sees — the theodicy of faith that his seeing leads to acting.
3.51: Eini 'ollela lenefshy mikol benot 'iri (My eye affects/grieves my soul because of all the daughters of my city). 'Ollela (H5953, to deal severely with/to afflict, from 'alal, to deal harshly) — the eye's witnessing of destruction itself produces grief in the nefesh. 'All the daughters of my city' (benot 'iri) — the plural 'daughters' as the communities/neighborhoods of Jerusalem, each representing a population affected. The poet's own nefesh (total self) is damaged by what the eyes have seen — the psychological reality of vicarious suffering, anticipating Ezekiel's 'I sat where they sat' (Ezek 3:15).
3.52: Tzod tzaduni kaytzippor oyevai chinnam (My enemies hunted me like a bird without cause). The tsade-section (vv 52-54) narrates the poet's experience of the pit. Tzad (to hunt, H6679) — used of hunting animals for food. Kaytzippor (like a bird, H6833) — the vulnerability of the hunted bird, caught in a snare (cf. Ps 124:7: our soul has escaped like a bird from the snare of the fowler). Chinnam (without cause, H2600) — the same word Ps 35:7, 19 use for David's enemies: they hid a net without cause; let them not rejoice who hate me without cause. The 'without cause' invokes the category of innocent suffering: the poet's hunting is not punishment for specific guilt but the unjust assault of enemies.
3.53: Tzamtu vavor chayyai vayyiddyu even bi (They have cut off my life in the pit and cast stones on me). Bor (H953, pit/cistern/dungeon) — one of the most theologically laden words in the OT: Joseph was thrown into a bor by his brothers (Gen 37:20-29); Jeremiah was thrown into a bor with mud (Jer 38:6); Ps 28:1 and 30:3 use the bor for Sheol, the realm of the dead. The pit is simultaneously a physical imprisonment and a figure for death and Sheol. The stone-throwing (yaddyu even) on the person in the pit recalls the stoning of prisoners. The combination of pit-imprisonment and stone-throwing creates the image of sealed entombment.
3.54: Tzafu mayim 'al roshi amarti nighzarti (The waters flowed over my head; I said, I am cut off). The flood-over-the-head image — drowning, overwhelmed by waters. Nighzarti (I am cut off, from gazar, H1504, to cut/divide) — the finality of the declaration. This is the deepest point of the poem: entombed in the pit, flooded by waters, the poet declares himself cut off. The same moment of apparent finality appears in Ps 31:22: 'I said in my alarm, I am cut off from your sight.' The declaration of being cut off is immediately followed by the appeal to YHWH (Ps 31:22b; Lam 3:55) — the moment of utter hopelessness is the moment of crying out.
3.55: Qarati shimcha YHWH mibor tachtioth (I called on your name, O LORD, from the depths of the pit). The qof-section (vv 55-57) is the turning point: from the pit, crying to YHWH. Bor tachtioth (H953 + H8482, the lowest pit) — the double intensification: the pit, and the lowest part of the pit. The divine name (shimcha YHWH, your name, YHWH) is invoked from the maximum depth — the same structure as Jonah 2 (from the belly of Sheol, Jonah prays and YHWH hears). The prayer from the pit to YHWH is the climax and reversal of v44 (your cloud blocks prayer) — what seemed impossible (prayer getting through the cloud) is here enacted: the poet prays from the pit and YHWH will answer.
3.56: Qoli shama'ta al ta'alem oznicha leravchati leshav'ati (You have heard my voice; do not close your ear to my plea, to my cry for relief). The shift to the past tense assertion — qoli shama'ta (my voice, you heard it) — represents the rhetorical shift from petition to confidence: YHWH has already heard. Revach (H7305, relief/space/release, a hapax here) — the breathing-room of relief after constriction. The plea not to close the ear (al ta'alem) grounds the confidence in YHWH's character: the one who heard in the past will not close his ear in the future. The past hearing becomes the basis for present confidence.
3.57: Qaravta beyom eqra'eka amarta al tira (You came near on the day I called to you; you said, Do not fear). Qaravta (you came near, from qarav, H7126) — YHWH's drawing near in response to the cry. The divine assurance al tira (do not fear, H3372) — the most frequent divine speech act in the OT (appearing over 70 times). YHWH's nearness and his 'fear not' together represent the complete resolution of the crisis: the far God who covered himself with a cloud is now near; the terrifying God is the one who says 'do not fear.' This is the pattern of every theophany: approach produces fear; YHWH's address removes the fear.
3.58: Ravta Adonai rivei nafshi ga'alta chayyai (You have taken up my cause, O Lord; you have redeemed my life). The resh-section (vv 58-60). Rivah (to contend/plead the case, H7378) — legal terminology: YHWH as the advocate who pleads the case. Ga'alta chayyai (you have redeemed my life, from ga'al, H1350, the kinsman-redeemer) — the go'el vocabulary: the one who redeems what was forfeit or imprisoned by acting as the nearest kin. The go'el relationship (Boaz for Ruth; Job's living Redeemer, Job 19:25) here becomes YHWH's action for the poet: the one in the pit has been redeemed. The translation notes flag nefesh: 'my life/my self', the whole living person rather than a Greek-influenced immaterial soul.
3.59: Ra'ita YHWH 'avvatti shofta mishpati (You have seen, O LORD, my wrong; judge my cause). The appeal to YHWH as judge after he has redeemed (v58) — the redemption is the basis for the appeal. 'Avvatti (my wrong/injustice suffered, from 'awah, H5753, to act wrongly; here the wrong done to the poet) — the injustice that was perpetrated against him. Shofta mishpati (judge my cause) — the call for YHWH to act as judge on behalf of the one he has redeemed. The redeemed person still awaits vindication; the double act (redemption from the pit; judgment that vindicates) are distinguished.
3.60: Ra'ita kol nikmataim kol machshevetam li (You have seen all their vengeance, all their plots against me). The knowledge that YHWH has seen the enemies' plans is itself a form of comfort: the suffering was not invisible or ignored. Nikmah (H5360, vengeance/retaliation) — YHWH as the one who sees and will act. Machshevetam (their plots/plans, from chashav, H2803, to think/plan) — the deliberate, premeditated nature of the enemies' assault. The poet's appeal rests on the fact that YHWH sees the injustice — the witnessing God who will act as judge.
3.61: Shama'ta cherpatam YHWH kol machshevetam 'alay (You have heard their taunts, O LORD, all their plots against me). The shin-section (vv 61-63) repeats the appeal to YHWH's perception: heard and seen (v60-61). Cherpatam (H2781, their insults/reproaches) — the public shame instrument, the insult directed at a person to destroy their social standing. YHWH has heard even the private plots and the public taunts — the comprehensive witness of the divine observer who misses nothing. The appeal to YHWH's comprehensive knowledge counters the experience of abandonment: YHWH was not absent but fully present as witness even when he seemed absent as deliverer.
3.62: Siftei qamay vehegeyonam 'alay kol hayom (The lips of those who rise against me and their whispering against me all day long). The enemies' lips (safah, H8193) and their murmuring/whispering (haggayon, H1902, meditation/murmuring) all day long — the constant, sustained verbal assault. The 'all day long' (kol hayom) has appeared multiple times in ch. 3 as the emphasis on the relentlessness of the suffering: all day the hand turned against him (v3), all day the mockery (v14), all day the scheming (v62). The repetition creates the texture of unrelenting assault that has no pause or relief.
3.63: Shivtam vequmam habbitah ani neginataim (Behold their sitting and rising; I am their mocking song). Shivtam uqumam (their sitting and rising) — the idiom for 'all their activities at all times' (cf. Deut 6:7: when you sit and when you rise). The poet is the subject of their mockery across all their activities, from sitting to rising. Neginatam (their mocking song, H5058 — the same word as v14's taunt-song) — the full circle: the enemies have made the poet the subject of their musical mockery from morning to night, in every posture.
3.64: Tashiv lahem gemul YHWH kema'aseh yedeihem (Give them their due, O LORD, according to the work of their hands). The tav-section (vv 64-66) is the imprecatory close — the call for divine justice against the enemies. Gemul (H1576, recompense/payment, from gamal, to deal out/repay) — the lex talionis principle: let what they did be done to them. The appeal is to YHWH as the judge who enforces the moral order: the one who sees (vv 59-63) will also act. The imprecatory prayer is grounded in confidence in divine justice rather than personal revenge.
3.65: Titten lahem meginnat lev ta'alatcha lahem (Give them hardness of heart, your curse for them). Meginnat lev (hardness/stubbornness of heart, H4044 + H3820 — a rare compound) — the closing up of the heart that prevents repentance, the judicial hardening that YHWH applies to those whose wickedness is complete (cf. Exod 4:21; Isa 63:17). Ta'alatcha (your curse, H8381, from ta'ala, to curse — a rare word) — the formal covenant curse invoked against those who have violated covenant. The double request (hardness of heart + curse) represents the complete shut-off of possibility: no repentance, and the curse fully in force.
3.66: Tirdof be'af vetashmid mitachat shemei YHWH (Pursue them in anger and destroy them from under your heavens, O LORD). The closing verse returns to the language of pursuit (radaf, H7291, to chase/pursue — used of YHWH pursuing Israel in judgment, Deut 28:45, and now invoked against Israel's enemies). Mitachat shemei YHWH (from under the heavens of YHWH) — the destruction is complete: the enemies are removed from the whole created order that YHWH governs. The poem ends not with comfort but with the confident invocation of divine judgment — the trust that YHWH will vindicate having been established in vv 21-60, the closing call for judgment expresses that same trust in its judicial form.

5.1: zekor YHWH meh hayah lanu: 'Remember, O LORD, what has happened to us.' The chapter opens with an imperative petition: zekor (remember, take notice, act). Throughout the OT, divine 'remembering' is not merely cognitive recall but saving action — YHWH remembered Noah (Gen 8:1), remembered Hannah (1 Sam 1:19), remembered the covenant (Exod 2:24). The petition here is: 'Act now on what you know of our condition.' The matching counterpart comes at v20: 'Why have you forgotten us forever?' — the prayer is framed by the contrast between what memory-as-action should do and what YHWH's silence seems to demonstrate. Habbita ure'eh et cherpateinu: 'Look and see our disgrace [cherpah].' Cherpah is the term for public shame, the reproach that falls on those abandoned by God and defeated before enemies (cf. 2:15; Jer 20:8; Ps 22:6). Chapter 5 is not an acrostic (unlike chs. 1-4) — it has 22 verses matching the Hebrew alphabet but no alphabetic ordering. The formal structure collapses into raw petition; artistic discipline gives way to direct prayer.
5.2: nachalateinu nehavekah lezarim batteinu lenokrim: 'Our inheritance has been given over to strangers, our houses to foreigners.' Nachalah (inheritance) is the covenantal term for Israel's portion of the land — the land YHWH gave to the fathers as a permanent possession (Deut 4:21; Josh 11:23). The transfer of nachalah to strangers is not merely an economic catastrophe; it is the dissolution of the covenant sign. Land tenure in Israel was theological: it confirmed YHWH's ongoing commitment to the Abrahamic promise. To lose the land to foreigners is to have the covenant promise publicly revoked. Zarim (strangers) and nokrim (foreigners) are both terms of alienness — those with no claim on Israel's covenant or territory.
5.3: yetomim hayinu vein av imotenu kealmanot: 'We have become fatherless orphans; our mothers are as widows.' The social categories here — yetomim (orphans, fatherless) and almanot (widows) — were Israel's most vulnerable people, protected by covenant law (Deut 24:17-21; Isa 1:17). The community now inhabits the condition of those at the bottom of the covenant social order. 'Fatherless' (yetomim) is not only social; it is theological — YHWH is the 'Father of the fatherless' (Ps 68:5), so that Israel's experience of fatherlessness implicitly cries out to YHWH as the only one who can fill that role. The widow-and-orphan imagery recurs from 1:1 (Jerusalem is like a widow) — the city and its people share the same bereaved condition.
5.4: meimeinu bekesef shatimu etzeinu bimhir yavou: 'We pay money for the water we drink; our wood must be bought at cost.' Water and wood — the most basic necessities of life in an agrarian society — are now commodities the community must purchase rather than draw freely from their own land and forests. In the covenant framework, the land's provision was a sign of YHWH's blessing; the Deuteronomic curse was removal of that provision (Deut 28:23-24: YHWH would make the sky bronze and the earth iron, withholding rain). The community pays strangers for what the land used to give freely, enacting the Deuteronomic curse in daily economic life. Bekesef (for silver/money) and bimhir (at cost/price) emphasize the transactional reversal: blessing used to be free gift; now it is a commodity bought from occupiers.
5.5: al tzavarenu nirdafnu yaganu velo huna lanu: 'Our pursuers are at our necks; we are driven hard with no rest.' The image of oppressors at the tzavar (neck) is the posture of domination — a conqueror's foot or yoke on the neck of the conquered (cf. Josh 10:24; Isa 10:27). This 'yoke on the neck' language inverts the Exodus: YHWH broke Egypt's yoke from Israel's neck (Lev 26:13); now Israel bears a yoke again. Lo huna lanu: 'No rest is given to us' — manoach (rest) was the gift of the land (Deut 12:9-10; Josh 21:44). The restlessness of exile enacts the covenant curse: 'among these nations you will find no repose, and there shall be no resting place for the sole of your foot' (Deut 28:65).
5.6: Mitzraim natanu yad Ashur liso'a lachem: 'We gave our hand to Egypt and to Assyria in order to get enough bread.' Natan yad (gave the hand) is an idiom for entering political submission or alliance — extending the hand in fealty (cf. 1 Chr 29:24; 2 Chr 30:8). The irony is devastating: Israel sought bread from Egypt and Assyria — the very powers YHWH had liberated them from or warned them against. The prophets repeatedly condemned such foreign alliances as covenant unfaithfulness (Isa 31:1: 'Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help'; Hos 7:11; Ezek 23). In seeking bread from these empires, Israel enacts the reversal of the Exodus and affirms what the exile demonstrated: they chose the bread of subjugation over the manna of covenant dependence.
5.7: avoteinu chatu veinam anachnu avonoteyhem savulnu: 'Our fathers sinned and are gone; we carry the weight of their iniquities.' This verse engages the deepest problem of exile theodicy: transgenerational guilt. The community does not claim personal innocence but notes the bitterness of bearing the weight (savulnu, we have borne/carried) of what others originated. The tension with Ezekiel's contemporaneous teaching (Ezek 18:20: the soul who sins shall die; the son shall not suffer for the father's iniquity) is real and intentional — Ezekiel insists on individual accountability; Lamentations acknowledges the historical reality that communities bear accumulated consequences. Avonoteyhem (their iniquities) — the same word used in Isa 53:11, where the Servant 'bears' (yisbol) iniquities: a significant lexical echo that the NT reads as Christ bearing what was not his own.
5.8: avadim mashlu vanu poreh ein miyyadam: 'Slaves rule over us; there is no one to rescue us from their power.' Avadim (slaves/servants) in positions of rule is a profound social inversion — Proverbs 30:22 lists a slave becoming king as one of four things that make the earth tremble. Ein poreh: 'no one delivers' — paraq means to snatch away, rescue violently (cf. Ps 136:24: YHWH 'snatched us from our foes'). The divine Rescuer who tore Israel from Egypt has not appeared; there is no deliverer from the Babylonian yoke. The absence of a human deliverer points to the need for one YHWH will provide — the go'el (kinsman-redeemer) theology that underpins the NT's presentation of Christ as the one who rescues where no human rescuer exists.
5.9: benafsheinu navi lachmeinu mippene cherev hamidbar: 'We risk our lives to get our bread, because of the sword that threatens in the open country.' Benafsheinu: 'at the cost of our nephesh' — the nephesh (life-breath, animate self) is placed at risk in the simple act of gathering food. The open country (hamidbar, the wilderness/field) that should provide food now threatens death. The image echoes the wilderness wandering: in the desert YHWH provided manna so Israel did not have to risk its life for food; now in a second exile-wilderness, they risk everything for bread. The perversion of the basic provision-of-bread that YHWH designed reflects the total reversal of covenant blessing.
5.10: oreinu ketannur nichtamu mippene zal'afot ra'av: 'Our skin burns hot as an oven because of the fever brought on by famine.' Zal'afot (fever, scorching wind, burning blast) is a rare term (cognate with zal'afah in Ps 11:6) suggesting the kind of burning heat associated with divine judgment as well as physical fever. Famine's physical effects — the skin darkening and tightening against the bones — are described in clinical detail throughout Lamentations (1:11; 2:11-12; 4:7-8). The tannur (oven/furnace) image connects to prophetic judgment language (Mal 4:1: the day is coming, burning like an oven) — here the community's physical suffering enacts in their bodies the judgment that fell on the city.
5.11: nashim beTziyon innu betulot be'arei Yehudah: 'Women have been violated in Zion, young women in the towns of Judah.' Innu (they violated, they humiliated) is the verb for sexual violence — the same root used of Tamar's rape (2 Sam 13:12), the Levite's concubine (Judg 19:24), and Dinah (Gen 34:2). The violation of betulot (young women, virgins) in Zion — the holy city — is part of Lamentations' unflinching account of total defeat. The protection of women was one of the duties of men and community in the ancient world; the inability to protect signals total social collapse. The violation of Zion's women is also the defilement of the holy city itself — the city that was supposed to be inviolable under YHWH's protection (Ps 46:5) is violated along with its inhabitants.
5.12: sarim beyadam nitlu pene zekenim lo nehddaru: 'Princes have been hanged by their hands; no honor is shown to the faces of elders.' Sarim (princes, officials) hung by their hands likely describes public execution by impalement or exposure — a form of humiliation reserved for enemies and criminals, not nobility. The public disgrace of leadership is complete: neither the young authorities (sarim) nor the old (zekenim, elders) retain their dignity. In the ancient honor-shame culture, showing honor to the penim (face) of elders was a fundamental social obligation (Lev 19:32: 'honor the face of an old man'). The suspension of this basic norm signals the dissolution of all social order — the community has no functional authority structure left.
5.13: bachurim tachon nasa'u une'arim baeitz kashalu: 'Young men are set to work at the grinding stone; boys stagger under loads of wood.' The tachon (grinding stone) was women's and slaves' work in ancient Israel — forcing young men (bachurim, the prime of manhood, those eligible for military service) to grind grain is both economic exploitation and gender-role humiliation. Ne'arim (boys, young men) carrying heavy wood burdens describes forced labor in Babylonian imperial projects. The inversion is complete: those who should be bearing weapons bear millstones and wood loads. The systematic degradation of each social class and age group (elders at v12, young men and boys at v13) constitutes the picture of total social disintegration.
5.14: zekenim mishsha'ar shavatu bachurim minneginatam: 'The elders have left the city gate; the young men have abandoned their music.' The sha'ar (city gate) was the center of civic life — the place where legal cases were heard (Ruth 4:1-11; Deut 21:19), commerce conducted, and elders governed. Its abandonment by the zekenim (elders) signals the end of ordered community life. Music — the neginah (string music, song) of young men — was associated with celebration, worship, and communal joy (cf. Ps 33:2-3; Amos 5:23). The double silence — legal authority gone, cultural expression gone — describes a community in which both civic order and cultural life have ceased entirely. The gate-as-place-of-judgment will be restored in the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:25: its gates will never be shut).
5.15: shavat masos libbenu nehpakh le'evel mecholenu: 'The joy of our hearts has ended; our dancing has turned into mourning.' Masos (joy, exuberant delight) is the term Jeremiah used when announcing the curse: YHWH would silence in Judah's cities the voice of mirth and gladness (Jer 7:34; 16:9; 25:10). Lamentations 5:15 is that prophecy's fulfillment: the masos has ceased. Machol (dancing) is elsewhere the instrument of Israel's praise and celebration of YHWH's saving acts (Ps 149:3; 150:4). The inversion — dancing (machol) to mourning (evel) — is the exact reversal of the covenant blessing; Jeremiah 31:13 promises YHWH will 'turn their mourning into joy' and 'their sorrow into gladness' as part of the new covenant restoration.
5.16: nafela ateret roshenu oy na lanu ki chatanu: 'The crown has fallen from our head. Woe to us — we have sinned!' Ateret (crown, wreath) symbolizes honor, dignity, and divine favor throughout the prophets. Its 'falling' (nafal) is the physical enactment of the loss of status; the community has been stripped of every marker of dignity. The critical movement of this verse is the turn to confession: oy na lanu ki chatanu — 'Woe to us, for we have sinned.' This is Lamentations' most direct statement of communal sin and guilt. The book has moved through grief (chs. 1-2), individual lament and hope (ch. 3), divine wrath and community shame (ch. 4), and arrives here at explicit confession. The fallen crown and the word chatanu (we have sinned) are the theological hinge of the closing prayer: the community owns the cause of its condition before petitioning for restoration.
5.17: al zeh hayah daveh libbenu al elleh chasheku einenu: 'Because of this our heart has grown faint; for all these things our eyes have become dim.' The physical symptoms of grief — daveh lev (faint/sick heart) and chashaku einayim (dimmed eyes) — are recognized in the OT as expressions of profound mourning and suffering. Davah (faint, sick) is the same root used in Isa 1:5 of Israel's sick heart and in Lam 1:22 of the individual lamenter's heart. Dimming eyes are associated with grief for the dead (Job 17:7; Ps 6:7) and with age. The verse presents the community as visibly wasting under the weight of accumulated loss — the physical is the register of the spiritual and communal suffering. 'Because of this' and 'for all these things' tie the bodily symptoms to the enumerated losses of vv. 1-16.
5.18: al har Tziyon sheshamem shu'alim hilechu vo: 'On account of Mount Zion, which lies desolate, jackals prowl across it.' Tziyon sheshamem: Zion desolate — the temple mount, YHWH's chosen dwelling place (Ps 132:13-14), now abandoned and inhabited by shu'alim (foxes/jackals). In the ancient world, wild animals colonizing abandoned cities was the ultimate sign of divine judgment and human absence; the prophets use this image explicitly (Isa 34:13-14 of Edom; Isa 13:21-22 of Babylon). The jackals on Zion invert the divine presence: where YHWH once dwelt in the temple, scavenging animals now roam. The verse prepares for the pivot to YHWH's eternal throne at v19 — the contrast is deliberately absolute: Zion completely desolate vs. YHWH's throne completely unaffected.
5.19: atah YHWH le'olam teshev kisekha ledor vador: 'But you, O LORD, reign forever; your throne endures through all generations.' The theological pivot. Against the backdrop of total desolation — Zion in ruins, jackals on the temple mount — the prayer suddenly affirms the one thing that has not changed: YHWH's eternal kingship. Le'olam (forever, for the age) and ledor vador (from generation to generation) are the terms of divine permanence, contrasted with the destruction of everything human and temporal in vv. 1-18. The same formula appears in Ps 102:12 (a psalm which, like Lamentations, moves from Zion's desolation to YHWH's eternal reign and ends with prayer for restoration). The verse functions as the theological anchor: the lament is not addressed to an absence but to a King who remains, however silent. YHWH's throne (kise) endures when David's throne has fallen — pointing to the one whose throne endures by inheriting YHWH's eternal kingship (Luke 1:33).
5.20: lama lanetzach tishkacheinu ta'azveinu le'orek yamim: 'Why have you forgotten us forever? Why have you forsaken us for such a long time?' The 'why' questions of lament (lama) are a standard form in the psalms of complaint (Ps 22:1; 44:23-24; 74:1). Two near-synonyms for divine abandonment are used: tishkacheinu (you forget us) and ta'azveinu (you forsake us). Shakach (to forget) is precisely what the petitioner asked YHWH NOT to do in v1 (zekor, remember) — the prayer is framed by desired memory vs. perceived forgetting. Azav (to forsake, abandon) is used of YHWH's relationship with Israel in Deut 31:6, 8 with the promise that YHWH will never forsake — making its use here as a question about YHWH's actual behavior all the more searching. Le'orek yamim: 'for so long a time' — the duration of exile makes divine forgetfulness seem like something more than temporary absence.
5.21: hashivenu YHWH elekha venashuva chadesh yameinu kekedem: 'Restore us to yourself, O LORD, that we may be restored. Renew our days as in former times.' The famous teshuvah verse of Lamentations. Hashivenu is the Hiphil (causative) of shuv (to return, repent, be restored) — 'cause us to return.' The matching venashuva (and we will return/be restored) completes the covenant dynamic: YHWH must initiate the return because the community cannot accomplish it on their own. Jeremiah uses this formula as part of the new covenant promise (Jer 31:18: 'Restore me, and I will be restored' — the same causative/intransitive pattern). The prayer recognizes that covenant restoration is not human achievement but divine gift: YHWH must first move, then the community can respond. Chadesh yameinu kekedem: 'renew our days as of old' — appealing to the Exodus-era relationship as the model for restoration. Jewish liturgical practice reads this verse a second time after v22, insisting on ending with hope rather than desolation.
5.22: ki im maos me'astanu qatzafta aleinu ad me'od: 'Or have you utterly rejected us? Are you exceedingly angry with us without end?' Lamentations closes on one of the Bible's most agonizing open questions. Ki im can introduce a strong contrary ('unless') or a resigned alternative ('or is it that...'). Maos me'as: the doubling of the verb (infinitive absolute + perfect) is the Hebrew idiom for complete, thoroughgoing action — 'utterly rejected' with no remainder. This is the question the community cannot answer: has YHWH's anger (qetzef, the intense divine wrath) passed its limit? The book ends without resolution — unlike most lament psalms, which move through petition to praise or assurance. The unresolved ending is the book's own theological act: exile is not resolved by literary closure; the community lives in the open question. Theologically, the open question of v22 is answered by the canon: the book is located within a Scripture that will ultimately declare that YHWH's rejection is not total — the resurrection of Christ is the definitive answer to whether divine anger is 'without end.'

1.1: Eikhah yashvah vadad ha-ir -- 'How the city sits alone!' The book opens with eikhah (H349), the particle of lamentation -- 'How!' or 'Alas!' -- used also in Isa 1:21 ('How the faithful city has become a harlot!') and Jer 48:17. It is also the book's Hebrew name. The word is not a question but an exclamation of grief at reversal: the formerly great city now sits vadad (alone, isolated, desolate). Rabati am hayetah ke-almanah -- 'she who was great among the nations has become like a widow.' The widow (almanah) is the most vulnerable social category in ancient Israel -- without male protection, without status, without provision. Jerusalem's greatness has been stripped down to this condition. The verse initiates the first aleph-bet acrostic (aleph = the first letter).
1.2: Bakho tivkeh ba-layil -- 'She weeps bitterly in the night.' The infinitive absolute bakho before the finite verb tivkeh intensifies: she weeps and weeps, continuously and desperately. Ve-dimatah al lhyah -- 'and her tears are on her cheeks.' The tears flow without being wiped away -- the image of grief so deep it goes unattended. Ein-lah menachem mi-kol-ohaveyha -- 'she has no comforter from all her lovers.' The first occurrence of the menachem (H5162, comforter) refrain that dominates ch 1. The 'lovers' (ohavim) are the political allies -- Egypt, Assyria, the surrounding nations -- who promised protection but have abandoned her. Kol-reyha bagdu vah -- 'all her friends have dealt treacherously with her' -- bagad (to betray/act treacherously), the vocabulary of covenant betrayal.
1.3: Galtah Yehudah me-oni u-me-rov avodah -- 'Judah has gone into exile because of affliction and because of great servitude.' Galah (H1540, to go into exile/uncover) -- the displacement of the covenant people from their land. Oni (H6040, affliction/misery) and avodah (H5656, labor/servitude) are the conditions that drove the exile. Hi yoshevet baggoyim lo matzaah manoach -- 'she dwells among the nations; she finds no resting place.' Manoach (resting place) -- the same word used in Ruth 3:1 for the security of marriage-home; Judah in exile has no such rest. Kol-rodfeha hisiguha bein ha-metzarim -- 'all her pursuers overtook her in the narrow places.' Metzarim (narrow straits/distress) -- also the word for Egypt (Mitzrayim); the exile involves a reversal to Egypt-like bondage.
1.4: Darkei Tziyyon avelot -- 'The roads of Zion mourn.' The personification extends: the roads themselves grieve because no pilgrim traffic moves on them. Mi-bli vai moed -- 'because none come to the appointed feast.' Moed (H4150, appointed time/feast/assembly) -- the great pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Shavuot, Sukkot) that filled the roads with worshippers have ceased. Kol-sharehah shomemot -- 'all her gates are desolate.' Shomemot (desolate/appalled) -- the verb used for the desolation of the Temple in Dan 9:27. Kohanehah neenaichim -- 'her priests groan.' The priests who led the cult now lead the grief. Betulotehah nugot ve-hi mar-lah -- 'her virgins are afflicted and she herself is bitter.' Betulot (young women/virgins) -- the most vulnerable of the population, representing both future hope and present desolation.
1.5: Hayu tzarehah le-rosh -- 'Her enemies have become the head.' Tzarehah (her enemies/adversaries) has inverted the covenant promise of Deut 28:44 -- the covenant curse is that the enemy will be the head and Israel the tail when Israel disobeys. Oyvehah shalu -- 'her foes prosper.' Shavah (to prosper/be at peace) -- the peace that was Israel's covenant gift now belongs to the oppressor. Ki-YHWH hogah al rov-pishaeyha -- 'because YHWH has afflicted her for the abundance of her transgressions.' Hogah (to afflict/grieve, H3013) -- the divine action is named clearly: the enemy's victory is YHWH's instrument. Pishaeyha (her transgressions, H6588) -- covenant violations, not merely moral failures. Olaleyha halekhu shevi lifnei tzar -- 'her children have gone into captivity before the enemy.' The children in exile is the sharpest image of total loss.
1.6: Vayetze mi-bat-Tziyyon kol-hadarah -- 'And gone from Daughter Zion is all her majesty.' Hadar (H1926, majesty/splendor/honor) -- the royal dignity and sacred glory associated with Jerusalem and the Temple. Its departure is the inverse of the kavod YHWH (glory of YHWH) departing the Temple in Ezekiel 10-11. Hayu sareha ke-ayalim lo matzeu mirheh -- 'her princes have become like deer that find no pasture.' The simile: deer (ayalim) without pasture are desperate, directionless, and doomed. Princes who should lead are now like animals fleeing a wasteland. Vayelekhu velo-koach lifnei rodeif -- 'and they fled without strength before the pursuer.' The exhaustion of total defeat.
1.7: Zakhrah Yerushalayim yemei onyah u-merudeha -- 'Jerusalem remembers in the days of her affliction and her wandering.' Zekhirah (H2142, to remember) -- memory is both gift and torment: she remembers what she has lost. Kol machamudeha asher hayu mi-yemei qedem -- 'all the precious things she had from days of old.' Machmudim (H4261, precious/desirable things) -- the Temple treasures, the liturgy, the peace. Bi-nefol ammah be-yad tzar ve-ein ozer lah -- 'when her people fell into the hand of the enemy and there was no helper.' Ozer (H5826, helper) -- another dimension of the abandoned woman; she cries out and no one answers. Rauha tzarim sachaku al-mishbateyha -- 'the enemies looked on her and mocked at her downfall.' The public shame of defeated Jerusalem witnessed by her captors.
1.8: Chet chatah Yerushalayim -- 'Jerusalem has sinned greatly.' The doubled construction (cognate accusative) intensifies: she has sinned a sin, she has grossly transgressed. Al-ken le-nidah hayetah -- 'therefore she has become an object of abhorrence.' Niddah (H5079, menstrual impurity/thing to be put away) -- the ritual category of separation. Jerusalem's sin has rendered her ritually impure and therefore socially excluded, the opposite of the holy city at the center of the nations. Kol-mechabbedehah hizziluha -- 'all who honored her despise her, for they have seen her nakedness.' The reversal: those who gave honor now give contempt. Ervah (H6172, nakedness) -- exposure and shame; also the legal language of sexual scandal and covenant violation (Deut 24:1).
1.9: Tumah be-shulehah -- 'Her uncleanness was in her skirts.' Tumah (H2932, impurity/uncleanness) -- ceremonial defilement. The metaphor of pollution in the hem of the garment represents the accumulation of sin that had become visible but unaddressed. Lo zakhrah acharitah -- 'she did not consider her end.' Acharit (H319, end/latter part/outcome) -- the same word Proverbs uses for wisdom's foresight: the fool does not consider how things will end. Jerusalem lacked the wisdom to foresee the consequences of covenant violation. Vattered plaim ein menachem lah -- 'she has fallen terribly; there is no one to comfort her.' The second menachem refrain. Reh YHWH et-onyah -- 'Look, O YHWH, at my affliction!' The first direct address to YHWH, breaking from third-person description to first-person appeal.
1.10: Yado parash tzar -- 'The enemy spread out his hand.' Parash yad (to spread the hand) -- the gesture of plundering; the enemy's hand extended over Jerusalem's treasuries. Al kol-machamudeha -- 'over all her precious things.' The machmudim return from v.7 -- Temple vessels, sacred objects, royal treasuries -- all now handled by the enemy's plundering hand. Ki ratah goyim bau mikdashah -- 'for she saw the nations enter her sanctuary.' The ultimate desecration: Gentiles entering the qodesh (holy place) that was forbidden even to many Israelites. Asher tzivvitah lo-yavou vaqahal lakh -- 'those whom you commanded not to enter your assembly.' The Torah's exclusion of certain nations from the assembly (Deut 23:2-9) has been violated by the very fact of conquest.
1.11: Kol-ammah neenaichim -- 'All her people groan.' Neanom (H584, to sigh/groan) -- the sound of desperate need, the moan that precedes prayer. Mevaqshim lachem -- 'seeking bread.' The famine that accompanied the siege (2 Kings 25:3; Jer 52:6) -- the most basic human need unmet. Natenu machamudehem be-okhel le-hashiv nafesh -- 'they give their precious things for food to revive their strength.' The machmudim (precious things) appear a third time -- now traded for survival food. What was sacred becomes mere barter. Re eh YHWH ve-habbitah ki hayiti zelulaah -- 'Look, O YHWH, and see, for I have become worthless.' The second direct address to YHWH -- the cry intensifies.
1.12: Lo aleikhem kol-ovrei derekh -- 'Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by?' The most famous verse in Lamentations. The Hebrew lo aleikhem (not to you) is literally: 'nothing to you?' -- a challenge to the indifferent passerby. Jerusalem appeals to witnesses. Habitu u-reu im-yesh makhov ke-makhovi asher olal li -- 'Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow, which was brought upon me.' Makhov (H4341, pain/sorrow) -- the word for grief that goes to the depths of one's being. The claim is that Jerusalem's suffering is unique in its severity. Asher hoga YHWH be-yom charon appo -- 'which YHWH inflicted on the day of his fierce anger.' The divine agency is again named; the suffering is not random but the result of YHWH's furious, purposeful action.
1.13: Mi-marom shalach-esh be-atzmotai -- 'From on high he sent fire into my bones.' The divine fire is not metaphorical -- it describes the destruction's violence reaching into the marrow of Jerusalem's life. Atzmot (bones) -- frequently used for the essential core of one's being (Ps 6:3; 31:11; Ezek 37). The fire from above (YHWH) burned downward into the inmost structure of the city and people. Vayirdenah -- 'and it subdued them.' Peras reshet le-raglai -- 'He spread a net for my feet.' The net imagery (H7568) -- used of hunters trapping prey; YHWH as the one who set the trap for Jerusalem's escape. Heshipani achoranit -- 'he turned me back.' Natani shomemah kol-hayom dava -- 'he has made me desolate and faint all the day long.'
1.14: Nishqad ol pashai be-yado -- 'The yoke of my transgressions was bound.' Nishqad (to bind tightly/to be wakeful over) -- an unusual verb suggesting careful, deliberate binding. Pesha (H6588, transgression) -- the same strong covenant-violation term as v.5. The transgressions are not loose moral failures but a yoke -- ol (H5923) -- bound onto Jerusalem's neck by YHWH's own hand. Yishtargu alu al-tzavvari -- 'they are woven together; they go up upon my neck.' The intertwining of the sins into a yoke suggests their accumulated, integrated character -- not isolated acts but a pattern of covenant violation. Hikhshil kochi -- 'he has made my strength fail.' The inevitable result of bearing the yoke of judgment.
1.15: Sillah kol-abirai YHWH be-kirbi -- 'The Lord rejected all my mighty men in my midst.' Sillah (H5541, to set aside/toss away) -- a contemptuous discarding. The military strength of Jerusalem -- its elite warriors -- was cast aside by YHWH as ineffective. Qara alai moed -- 'he summoned an assembly against me.' The tragic irony: moed normally denotes the sacred festival assembly of worship; here YHWH calls a moed against Jerusalem -- a military assembly of destruction rather than a liturgical assembly of praise. Lishbor bachurai -- 'to crush my young men.' The youth who should have been the future were crushed. Gat darak YHWH le-vetulat bat-Yehudah -- 'The Lord has trodden the virgin Daughter Judah as in a winepress.' The winepress image (gat) -- harvest fruit crushed underfoot -- applied to the people of Judah. A reversal of vineyard imagery from Isaiah.
1.16: Al-elleh ani vokhiyah -- 'For these things I weep.' The first-person voice of the personified city intensifies. Eini eini yeredah mayim -- 'my eye, my eye runs down with water.' The doubled eini emphasizes the uncontrollable flow of tears. Ki rachaq mimmenni menachem -- 'because far from me is the comforter.' The third menachem refrain -- but now the comforter's absence is described spatially: rachaq (far, distant). The one who should be near is unreachably far. Meshiv nafshi -- the comforter who would 'restore my soul' (same phrase as Ps 23:3 -- YHWH the shepherd restoring his flock). Hayu vanai shomemim ki gavar ha-oyev -- 'my children are desolate because the enemy has prevailed.'
1.17: Pershah Tziyyon et-yadehah ein menachem lah -- 'Zion spreads out her hands; there is no comforter for her.' The fourth and climactic menachem refrain in ch 1. Pershah yadehah -- the gesture of prayer and petition, hands spread upward or outward in appeal. But the hands reach into emptiness; no comforter responds. Tzivvah YHWH le-Yaaqov sevivav tzarav -- 'YHWH has commanded Jacob's neighbors to be his enemies.' The theological weight: YHWH himself commanded the surrounding nations to become adversaries. The enemies are instruments of divine judgment, not autonomous aggressors. Hayetah Yerushalayim le-niddah beinetam -- 'Jerusalem has become an unclean thing among them.' The niddah exclusion of v.8 returns -- Jerusalem is quarantined from the community of nations by her ritual defilement of sin.
1.18: Tzaddiq hu YHWH -- 'The LORD is righteous.' In the middle of the lament, the city acknowledges divine justice: the destruction is not unjust. Ki fi mariti -- 'for I rebelled against his word.' Mariti (H4784, to rebel/be contentious) -- the admission of covenant rebellion. This verse is the theological hinge of ch 1: Jerusalem's theological confession within the lament. She does not protest her innocence but admits her guilt. Shimu-na kol-ha-ammim ve-reu makhovi -- 'Hear, all peoples, and behold my sorrow.' The appeal to witness expands beyond passersby (v.12) to all nations. Betulotai u-vachurei halekhu vashevi -- 'my young women and young men have gone into captivity.' The youth -- the future -- in exile.
1.19: Qarati le-meahavai hemah rimmuni -- 'I called to my lovers; they deceived me.' Rimmuni (H7411, deceived/betrayed) -- the political allies who promised security have proved false. Kohanai u-zeqenai ba-ir gave u -- 'my priests and elders perished in the city.' Those who were meant to intercede (priests) and to counsel (elders) are dead. The institutional leadership has collapsed. Ki viqqeshu okhel lamo ve-yashivu et-nafesham -- 'while they sought food to revive their strength.' Even the priests died in the siege-famine, searching for bread like everyone else. The liturgical community that sustained the nation's covenant life has dissolved.
1.20: Reh YHWH ki tzar li -- 'Look, O YHWH, for I am in distress.' The third direct address to YHWH. Tzar (H6862, narrow/distress) -- the same root as metzarim (v.3) -- constriction, the opposite of the spaciousness of covenant blessing. Meai chamaru -- 'my stomach churns.' Chamaru (H2560, to ferment/churn) -- the internal upheaval of grief and fear. Nefekh libbi alai -- 'my heart is overturned within me.' Nafakh (to be overturned/poured out) -- the dissolution of inner structure. Ki marod mariti -- 'for I have been very rebellious.' The repeated confession: twice in this chapter Jerusalem names her own rebellion as the cause. Mi-chutz shikhkhelah cherev ba-bayit ka-mavet -- 'Outside, the sword bereaves; inside, it is like death.' The siege has made outside and inside equally fatal.
1.21: Shamu ki neenaichti -- 'They heard how I am groaning.' Neanaichti (H584, I am groaning) -- the groan as both audible suffering and implicit prayer. Ein menachem li -- the fifth and final menachem refrain of ch 1. The entire first chapter is structured around this absence: the comforter who does not come. Kol-oyvai shameu raati sasu -- 'all my enemies heard of my trouble; they rejoiced.' Sasu (rejoiced/exulted) -- the enemy's delight at Jerusalem's fall, which will itself become a subject of lamentation and judgment. Heveita yom-qarata -- 'You brought on the day you announced.' The enemies' joy is within YHWH's announced plan.
1.22: Tavo kol-raatam lefanekha -- 'Let all their evil come before you.' The lament turns to imprecation -- a prayer for YHWH to deal with the enemies as he has dealt with Jerusalem. Ve-olel lamo ka-asher olalta li -- 'and deal with them as you have dealt with me for all my transgressions.' The final verse requests proportionate divine justice: the enemies, who have also transgressed, should face the same judgment Jerusalem has faced. Ki rabbim anachoti ve-libbi davvai -- 'for my groans are many and my heart is faint.' The book's first chapter ends not with resolution but with ongoing grief: the groaning continues, the heart remains sick. The lack of comforter with which the chapter opened is matched by the lack of comfort with which it closes -- an unresolved lament awaiting divine response.

2.1: Eikhah yaib Adonai be-appo -- 'How the Lord has covered Daughter Zion with his anger.' Ch 2 opens with the same eikhah as ch 1, but the perspective shifts dramatically: where ch 1 described the suffering primarily from Jerusalem's perspective, ch 2 focuses on YHWH as the active agent of destruction. Yaib (H5743, to cover with clouds/darken) -- YHWH's anger is like a storm cloud covering the city. Hishlik mi-shamayim eretz tiferet Yisrael -- 'he cast down from heaven to earth the splendor of Israel.' The tiferet (glory/beauty/splendor) of Israel -- likely the Temple and its associated glory -- is hurled downward. The fall is literally from heaven to earth, a reversal of the Temple's sacred orientation. Ve-lo zakhar hadom raglav be-yom appo -- 'and did not remember his footstool on the day of his anger.' The footstool (hadom raglav) of YHWH is the Temple (Ps 99:5; 132:7; 1 Chr 28:2) -- the place of his earthly throne-presence, now forgotten in wrath.
2.2: Billa YHWH ve-lo chamal -- 'The Lord swallowed up without mercy.' Billa (H1104, to swallow up/engulf) -- the verb of total destruction; nothing remains after swallowing. Lo chamal (he did not spare/had no mercy) -- the absence of the compassion (rachamim) that normally characterizes YHWH. Et kol-neot Yaaqov -- 'all the dwelling places of Jacob.' Neot (H4999, pastures/dwelling places) -- the inhabited land of the covenant people. Haras be-evrato mivtzarei bat-Yehudah -- 'he broke down in his wrath the strongholds of Daughter Judah.' Haras (H2040, to tear down/demolish) -- the military destruction of fortifications. The word appears repeatedly in this chapter. Higa la-aretz chilelah mamlakhtah ve-sareha -- 'he has brought down to the ground in dishonor the kingdom and its princes.' The royal institutions have been brought low.
2.3: Gada be-charon af kol-qeren Yisrael -- 'He cut off in fierce anger all the strength of Israel.' Gada (H1438, to cut off/fell) -- the verb for cutting down a tree; Israel's military strength is felled like timber. Qeren (H7161, horn) -- the symbol of power and strength; all of Israel's strength is cut at once. Heishiv achor yemino mifnei oyev -- 'he withdrew his right hand from before the enemy.' The yamin (right hand) is YHWH's weapon hand, his hand of saving power. Its withdrawal is the decisive moment: YHWH stops fighting for Israel and begins fighting against them. Vayivar be-Yaaqov ke-esh lehevah -- 'and he burned through Jacob like a flaming fire.' The divine fire imagery from ch 1:13 recurs -- but now the source (YHWH himself burning like a flame) is fully explicit.
2.4: Darakh qashto ke-oyev -- 'He bent his bow like an enemy.' The theological crisis of ch 2 reaches its apex: YHWH acts as oyev (enemy) against his own people.
