Nitzav yemino ke-tzar -- 'he set his right hand like a foe.' The same right hand that was Israel's salvation (Exod 15:6; Ps 118:15-16) is now set as an adversary. Va-yaharog kol machmadei ayin -- 'and he killed all who were precious to the eye.' Machmadim (precious things/beloved ones) -- the recurring word now for people rather than objects: the beloved ones whom YHWH himself slew. Be-ohel bat-Tziyyon shafakh ke-esh chamato -- 'in the tent of Daughter Zion he poured out his wrath like fire.' YHWH is not merely permitting the enemy's destruction but actively pouring out fury within the sacred precincts.
2.5: Hayah YHWH ke-oyev -- 'The LORD has become like an enemy.' The starkest theological statement in the book. Ke-oyev (like an enemy) -- the particle ke (like) is important: YHWH is compared to an enemy in his actions, not identified as one essentially. But the functional equivalence is real and devastating. Billa Yisrael -- 'he swallowed up Israel.' The billa of v.2 returns. Billa kol-armenotehah -- 'he swallowed up all her palaces.' Armenotot (H759, palaces/citadels) -- the structures of royalty and governance consumed. Ve-yarbeh be-bat-Yehudah taaniyah va-aniyah -- 'and he has multiplied mourning and lamentation in Daughter Judah.' Taaniyah (H8589, lamentation) and aniyah (H592, sighing/groaning) -- a pair of mourning terms.
2.6: Vayachmos ke-gan sukko -- 'He has stripped his booth like a garden.' Sukko (his booth/shelter) refers to the Temple as YHWH's dwelling place -- the same vocabulary as Ps 27:5 and 76:3. The garden stripping suggests violent clearance. Shichet moado -- 'he has destroyed his meeting place.' Moed again -- the appointed-feast assembly, but now it is YHWH who destroys his own meeting place. Hishkach YHWH be-Tziyyon moed ve-Shabbat -- 'YHWH has caused Zion to forget the appointed feast and the Sabbath.' Hishkach (to cause to forget) -- YHWH himself erases the liturgical calendar, the rhythm of covenant worship. The Sabbath and festival cycle that structured Israel's life is suspended. Vayinatz be-zaam appo melekh ve-kohen -- 'and in his angry indignation he has spurned king and priest.' Both the royal and priestly institutions are objects of divine contempt.
2.7: Zanach YHWH mizbecho -- 'The Lord has rejected his altar.' Zanach (H2186, to reject/cast off) -- a strong term for absolute repudiation; the same verb used in Ps 77:8 for the fear that YHWH has forever cast off. The altar -- the central locus of Israel's sacrificial worship and atonement -- is rejected by the one to whom it was directed. Nir miqdash -- 'he has disowned his sanctuary.' Nir (H5010, to abhor/disown) -- the complete reversal of the Temple's consecration. Higger be-yad oyev chomat armenotehah -- 'he has delivered into the hand of the enemy the walls of her palaces.' What the covenant was meant to protect has been handed over to the very enemy it was meant to exclude. Qol natenu be-veit-YHWH ke-yom moed -- 'they raised a shout in the house of the LORD as on the day of appointed feast.' The enemy's battle cry in the Temple sounds like a feast-day shout -- a terrible inversion of sacred sound.
2.8: Chashav YHWH le-hashchit chomat bat-Tziyyon -- 'The LORD determined to lay in ruins the wall of Daughter Zion.' Chashav (H2803, to plan/design/reckon) -- the same word used for skilled craftsmanship (Exod 31:4) and for theological recounting; here it is YHWH's deliberate architectural plan -- but for demolition. Natah qav lo-heishiv yado mi-balla -- 'he stretched out the measuring line; he did not restrain his hand from swallowing.' The qav (measuring line) is the builder's tool -- but here stretched out by a divine demolisher measuring what he will destroy (cf. Amos 7:7-9, the plumb line). Va-yaavel chel ve-chomah -- 'and he made rampart and wall mourn.' Even the fortification structures participate in the lament.
2.9: Tabeu va-aretz sharehah -- 'Her gates have sunk into the ground.' The gates -- the place of justice, commerce, and assembly -- are not merely broken but have sunk below the surface, the inverse of the city built on a hill. Ibad u-shiver berichehah -- 'he has destroyed and broken her bars.' The bars (berichin) that locked the gates for defense are shattered. Malkah ve-sareha baggoyim ein Torah -- 'her king and princes are among the nations; the Torah is no more.' The two institutions of covenant governance -- king and Torah -- are simultaneously lost: the king in exile, the Torah without teacher or Temple context to enact it. Gam-neviehah lo matzeu chazon me-YHWH -- 'also her prophets find no vision from YHWH.' The prophetic word has ceased -- the silence of YHWH is itself a form of judgment.
2.10: Yeshvu la-aretz yidmemu ziqnei bat-Tziyyon -- 'The elders of Daughter Zion sit on the ground in silence.' Yidmemu (H1826, to be silent/still/dumb) -- the silence that is the absence of wisdom's speech. The elders, whose role was counsel and judgment at the gate, sit mute in the dust. Alu afar al-rosham -- 'they have thrown dust on their heads.' The gesture of mourning: dust or ashes on the head (Josh 7:6; Job 2:12; Neh 9:1). Chatru saqim betulot Yerushalayim -- 'the virgins of Jerusalem have bowed their heads to the ground.' Chatru (bowed down/girded in sackcloth) -- the double gesture of grief: posture and garment. The book's most vulnerable figures -- the young women who represent future and fertility -- are prostrated in mourning.
2.11: Kalu ve-demaot einai -- 'My eyes are spent with weeping.' Kalu (H3615, to be spent/exhausted/completed) -- the eyes have reached their limit of tears. The observer's own weeping begins now, shifting from third-person description to first-person participation in the grief. Chamarmaru meai -- 'my stomach churns.' The same chamarmaru as 1:20 -- the physical manifestation of grief in the gut. Nishpakh la-aretz kevodi -- 'my bile is poured on the ground.' Kaved (liver/bile, H3516) -- the ancients considered the liver the seat of emotion; its pouring out = total emotional depletion. Al-shever bat-ammi -- 'because of the destruction of Daughter my-people.' Shever (H7667, breaking/destruction) -- a decisive breaking. The phrase bat-ammi (Daughter of my people) expresses intimate identification: not merely 'the people' but 'my people's daughter.'
2.12: Le-imotam yomeru ayeh dagan va-yayin -- 'They cry to their mothers: Where is bread and wine?' The particular horror of famine during siege: children crying for basic food. Dagan (grain/bread) and yayin (wine) -- the staples of covenant blessing (Deut 7:13; 11:14). Their absence represents the inversion of covenant promise. Be-hithatpam ke-chalal bi-rechovot ha-ir -- 'as they faint like the wounded in the streets of the city.' Chalal (H2491, wounded/slain) -- the children's fainting from hunger is compared to warriors fallen in battle. The domestic space of the city has become a battlefield. Be-hishta-peak nafesham el-cheq imotam -- 'as their soul is poured out into their mothers' bosoms.' The dying children collapse onto their mothers -- an image of total helplessness combined with the final refuge of maternal embrace.
2.13: Mah-aidekh mah-adammeh lakh ha-betulat bat-Tziyyon -- 'What can I bear witness against you? What can I compare to you, O virgin Daughter Zion?' The observer's question is not rhetorical mockery but genuine perplexity: there is no adequate comparison for this suffering. Ki-gadol ka-yam shivrekh -- 'for great as the sea is your ruin.' Shever (breaking/destruction, H7667) -- the same word as v.11. The comparison to the sea (yam) -- limitless, unmeasurable -- indicates that the catastrophe exceeds normal frameworks of comparison. Mi-yirpa lakh -- 'who can heal you?' Rafa (H7495, to heal) -- healing is the word of covenant restoration; but who can heal a wound of ocean dimensions?
2.14: Neviayikh chazu lakh shav va-tafel -- 'Your prophets have seen for you false and deceptive visions.' Shav (H7723, emptiness/falsehood/vanity) -- the same word used in the third commandment ('do not take the name of YHWH in vain' / la-shav); prophets who spoke in YHWH's name without divine authority. Tafel (H8602, insipid/daubed/whitewashed) -- false prophecy as whitewashing over cracks, giving a false impression of structural soundness. Ve-lo gilu al-avonekh le-hashiv shevutekh -- 'they did not expose your iniquity to restore your fortune.' True prophecy addresses sin in order to enable restoration; false prophecy avoids it and leaves the people without warning or opportunity for repentance. Vayechezu lakh massaot shav u-maduchim -- 'they saw false oracles and causes of banishment for you.' The false prophets' visions contributed to the exile they were supposed to prevent.
2.15: Sapequ alayikh kappayim kol-ovrei derekh -- 'All who pass by clap their hands at you.' Where 1:12 appealed to passers-by for sympathy, here the passers-by respond with mockery -- clapping hands (sapequ kappayim) as a gesture of contempt and derision. Sharqu vayanu rosham al bat-Yerushalayim -- 'they hiss and wag their heads at Daughter Jerusalem.' Sharqu (to hiss) -- a sound of scorn and shock. Hazot ha-ir asher yomeru kelilat yofi -- 'Is this the city that was called the perfection of beauty?' The quotation mocks the theological claim of Jerusalem's unique beauty (Ps 48:2 -- 'beautiful in elevation, the joy of all the earth'). What was acclaimed as perfect has been reduced to ruin. Mesos le-khol-ha-aretz -- 'the joy of all the earth' -- now an object of scorn rather than joy.
2.16: Patztu alayikh pihhem kol-oyvayikh -- 'All your enemies open their mouths against you.' Patztu pihhem (opened their mouth wide) -- the gaping mouth of contempt and triumph. Sharqu va-yachrequ shen -- 'they hiss and gnash their teeth.' Charaqu shen (gnashing teeth) -- the aggressive physical expression of hostility and derision (Ps 35:16; 37:12). Amru bilanu -- 'they say: We have swallowed her up.' The same bila verb used of YHWH's action in vv. 2, 5 -- now the enemies claim the same act as their own. Akh zeh ha-yom shequivvinu -- 'surely this is the day we looked for.' The enemies' long-anticipated opportunity has arrived. Matzanu rainu -- 'we found it; we saw it.' The gleeful confirmation of Jerusalem's destruction.
2.17: Asah YHWH asher zamam -- 'The LORD has done what he purposed.' Zamam (H2161, to plan/devise/purpose) -- YHWH's deliberate intention; nothing has happened accidentally. Bitza imrato asher tzivvah mi-mei qedem -- 'he has carried out his word that he commanded long ago.' The destruction is the execution of the covenant curses announced in Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 -- commands given at Sinai, centuries before their fulfillment. Haras ve-lo chamal -- 'he tore down without mercy.' The same lo chamal of v.2. Vayesamach alayikh oyev -- 'and he caused the enemy to rejoice over you.' YHWH is the cause even of the enemy's rejoicing -- a further dimension of the theological crisis. Herim qeren tzarayikh -- 'he exalted the horn of your adversaries.' The horn (power) that was supposed to belong to Israel (Ps 89:17) has been transferred to the enemy.
2.18: Tzaaq libam el-YHWH -- 'Their heart cried to the LORD.' Suddenly a call to prayer emerges. Chomat bat-Tziyyon -- 'O wall of Daughter Zion.' The wall itself is addressed as a mourner and intercessor. Horidi ka-nachal dimah yomam va-lailah -- 'let tears stream down like a river day and night.' Ka-nachal (like a wadi/stream) -- the image of tears as a never-stopping watercourse. Day and night (yomam va-lailah) -- total, unceasing lament. Al-titni fugat lakh -- 'give yourself no rest.' Fugat (H6314, respite/rest) -- the call to relentless prayer. Al-tidom bat-einekh -- 'let not your eye's daughter be still.' The 'daughter of your eye' -- the pupil -- should not stop weeping. The intensity of the call matches the severity of the crisis.
2.19: Qumi roni va-layl -- 'Arise, cry out in the night.' The call to night-prayer -- the watches of the night when grief is most acute and the cry most desperate. Le-rosh ashmurot -- 'at the beginning of the night watches.' The night was divided into three or four watches; the first watch (beginning of the night) is when prayer should begin, not wait. Shiphki ka-mayim libbekh nochach penei YHWH -- 'pour out your heart like water before the presence of the LORD.' Shaphak lev (to pour out the heart) -- total self-disclosure in prayer, holding nothing back. Nochach penei YHWH -- 'before the face of YHWH' -- the prayer is directed at the divine presence. Sei elav kappayikh -- 'lift your hands to him.' The gesture of prayer -- lifted palms. Al-nefesh olalelayikh -- 'for the souls of your young children who are fainting with hunger at every street corner.'
2.20: Reh YHWH ve-habbitah le-mi olalta koh -- 'Look, O LORD, and see! With whom have you dealt thus?' The direct appeal to divine witness. Habbitah (H5027, to look/gaze with attention) -- to look carefully, not casually. The question le-mi olalta koh (to whom have you done thus) is not a challenge to divine justice but a cry of bewilderment: this is your covenant people -- this is what has happened. Im-tokhal nashim piryam -- 'Should women eat the fruit of their womb?' The horror of cannibalism during the siege is introduced (cf. Deut 28:53-57; Jer 19:9; Ezek 5:10) -- the most extreme symbol of total social collapse. Im-yehareg be-miqdash YHWH kohen ve-navi -- 'should priest and prophet be killed in the sanctuary of the LORD?' The Temple, the last refuge, has become a killing floor.
2.21: Shakhvu la-aretz chutztot naar ve-zaqen -- 'In the dust of the streets lie young and old.' The dead lie unburied -- the extremity of desolation. Betulotai u-vachuray naflu ve-charev -- 'my young women and young men have fallen by the sword.' The young generation -- the future -- is slain. Haragtah be-yom appekha tavachta lo chamaltha -- 'you killed them in the day of your anger; you slaughtered without mercy.' The theological claim remains: YHWH is the agent. Tavachta (H2873, to slaughter/butcher) -- the vocabulary of the slaughterhouse applied to the divine action. Lo chamaltha -- the lo chamal of vv. 2, 17 -- three times in this chapter the absence of divine mercy is named. The covenant compassion (rachamim) has been suspended.
2.22: Tiqra ke-yom moed megorai mi-saviv -- 'You summoned as on a day of appointed feast my terrors from every side.' The final verse uses the moed motif one last time -- but reversed. YHWH calls together ('summons' like a festival assembly) Jerusalem's terrors (megorai, H4032, terrors/fears) from every direction. The feast-day convocation is now a gathering of horrors. Ve-lo hayah be-yom af-YHWH palith ve-sarid -- 'and on the day of the LORD's anger no one escaped or survived.' The totality of destruction -- palith (escapee) and sarid (survivor/remnant) -- both categories are emptied. Asher tiladti ve-ribbiti -- 'those whom I carried and raised.' The personified Jerusalem speaks as a mother whose children were her investment; they are all gone. Chapter 2 ends with no resolution, no comfort, no reversal -- only the enormity of what YHWH has done to his people standing naked before the reader.

4.1: Eich yukham zahav yishna hakethem hatov tishpaknah avnei qodesh bero'sh kol chutzot (How the gold has grown dim, how the pure gold has changed! The holy stones are scattered at the head of every street). Zahav (H2091, gold) and ketem (H3800, pure/fine gold, H6337 paz is used in v2) — the two Hebrew gold-words used in ch. 4 signal the best quality: zahav is general; ketem (H3800) and paz (H6337) denote the finest, most pure gold. The dimming of the finest gold is the opening metaphor for the degradation of what was most valuable in Jerusalem. The 'holy stones' (avnei qodesh) scattered in the streets — likely referring either to the temple stones or metaphorically to the temple personnel (priests and Levites) now reduced to wandering.
4.2: Benei Tzion hayeqarim hamesullaim bapaz eichah nechshavu lenvlei cheres ma'aseh yedei yotzer (The precious sons of Zion, worth their weight in fine gold — how they are regarded as earthen vessels, the work of a potter's hands!). Paz (H6337, fine gold, the purest grade) — the same word used in Ps 19:10 and 119:127 for the highest value. The sons of Zion weighed against fine gold — the maximum human value in ANE economic terms. The contrast with clay pots (nevlei cheres, earthen vessels) is the most extreme degradation possible: from gold-equivalent to disposable pottery. The potter's vessel imagery connects to Jer 18-19 (YHWH as potter, Israel as clay) — the sovereign remaking that Lamentations depicts as the destruction now needed for the future restoration.
4.3: Gam taninim chalitzu shad heniqu gurevihen bat 'ammi le'akzar kaya'enim bamidbar (Even jackals offer the breast; they nurse their young; but the daughter of my people has become cruel, like the ostriches in the desert). Taninim (H8577, jackals/sea-monsters/serpents — a contested word) — here most likely jackals or sea-animals that nurse their young; even predatory/wild creatures nurse their offspring. The bat 'ammi (daughter of my people) is more cruel than the jackals: the mother-Jerusalem has abandoned her children. The ostrich (ya'en, H3282, from ya'anah, H3284, the ostrich) in the desert — the proverbially cruel mother who abandons her eggs (Job 39:14-16: she leaves her eggs in the earth and forgets that a foot may crush them). The covenant community compared unfavorably to wild animals for lack of maternal care.
4.4: Davaq lashon yoneq el chikko batzama 'olelim sha'alu lechem poresh ein lahem (The tongue of the nursing infant clings to the roof of its mouth for thirst; the children beg for food, but no one gives to them). The imagery of thirst and hunger focused on the most vulnerable — nursing infants and small children. Davaq lashon (tongue clings to the palate, H1692 + H3956) — the physical sensation of dehydration, the tongue dry and stuck. Sha'alu lechem (begging for bread, from sha'al, H7592, to ask/beg) — active begging by children who have no food. The children's hunger was the ultimate condemnation of the failed society: a community that lets its children starve has lost the most basic form of social covenant.
4.5: Ha'okelim lema'adanim nashamu bachutzo ha'emunah 'al tola'at (Those who feasted on delicacies perish in the streets; those who were brought up in purple cling to ash heaps). Ma'adanim (H4516, delicacies/delights — from 'adan, to be delicate/luxurious) — the wealthy who ate fine food. Tolaath (H8438, scarlet/worm) — the color scarlet, dyed with the crimson worm; the royal/wealthy color of luxury garments (cf. the scarlet thread of Gen 38; the scarlet in the tabernacle; the Proverbs 31 woman who clothes her household in scarlet). The reversal: those in purple and scarlet are now clinging to the dunghills of destruction. The great reversal of Lamentations (the mighty brought low) mirrors the Magnificat's pattern (Luke 1:52-53).
4.6: Vayigdal avon bat 'ammi mechata't Sedom hahafucha kemo rega' velo chalu vah yadayim (For the punishment of the daughter of my people has been greater than the sin of Sodom, which was overthrown in a moment, when no hands were laid on her). The comparison to Sodom — the OT's paradigm of total divine judgment — but here reversed: Jerusalem's punishment is declared greater than Sodom's. The reason given: Sodom was destroyed in a moment (rega', H7281, an instant) without human hands; Jerusalem has endured prolonged suffering. The greater punishment suggests either greater sin or the greater covenant privilege and responsibility (Luke 12:48: to whom much is given, much will be required). The covenant context makes Jerusalem's judgment heavier because the covenant relationship made the violation more serious.
4.7: Zaku nezireha misheleg tzachu mechalav ademu peninim sappirum gizratam (Her princes were purer than snow, whiter than milk; their bodies more ruddy than coral, their hair like sapphire). Nezireha (H5139, her Nazirites/consecrated ones — or 'princes', if nazir is taken as the title for consecrated leaders rather than specifically Nazirite vow-keepers). The four-color sequence (snow-white, milk-white, coral-red, sapphire-blue) represents the ideal human appearance in the ancient Near East — each a prized material of highest quality. The Nazirites/princes are described in the aesthetic vocabulary of the most precious natural materials. The contrast with v8 makes the before/after starkest.
4.8: Chashak mishachor taram lo nikru bachutzo tzafad 'uram qotzef 'al 'atzam yavas hayah kaeitz (Now their faces are blacker than soot; they are not recognized in the streets; their skin has shriveled on their bones; it has become as dry as wood). The physical transformation of the formerly beautiful to the unrecognizable — faces blackened with soot and deprivation, skin shriveled on bones. Qotzef (H6821, to shrivel/wrinkle, a hapax legomenon) and yavas (H3001, to be dry/withered) create the texture of complete dehydration and emaciation. The unrecognizable (nikru, H5234, to recognize/acknowledge) — those who had been the embodiment of beauty are now unidentifiable. The irony: those who were the most distinctive in appearance are now the most anonymous in their suffering.
4.9: Tovim hayu challelei cherev mechallei ra'av sheyazubu medukkarim mitvenuat sadai (Better off were the victims of the sword than the victims of hunger, who waste away, stricken by lack of the fruits of the field). The grim calculus: a quick death by the sword is preferable to the slow death of starvation. Medukkarim (H1856, pierced/stabbed, from dakar) — those stabbed by the sword die quickly; those who starve (challelei ra'av, H2491 + H7458, pierced by famine — the same word, 'chalal', being both 'pierced' and 'profaned/slain') die slowly. The covenant curse of Deut 28:22-24, 53-57 specified famine as one of the most severe judgments precisely because of its gradual, agonizing character.
4.10: Yedei nashim rachmamaniyot bishelu yaldeihen hayu levarot lahem beshever bat 'ammi (The hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children; they became their food during the destruction of the daughter of my people). The most terrible image in Lamentations: nashim rachamaniyot (compassionate women) — the epithet deliberately highlights the contradiction. The women who are by nature (rachamim = compassion, from the womb-root rechem) the most nurturing are reduced by famine to eating their children. This fulfills the specific covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:56-57: even the most tender and delicate woman among you will begrudge her husband and her children the flesh of the children she bears, for she intends to eat them secretly. The text's citation of this specific covenant curse makes Lamentations the experiential fulfillment of Deuteronomy's warning.
4.11: Kilah YHWH et chamato shafach charon afo vayatzet esh beTziyon vatochal yesodoteiha (The LORD gave full vent to his wrath; he poured out his fierce anger, and he kindled a fire in Zion that consumed its foundations). Kilah (H3615, to finish/complete/bring to an end) — YHWH has completed his wrath. Shafach charon afo (poured out the burning of his nostrils/anger) — the outpouring-of-wrath vocabulary standard in prophetic judgment oracles (cf. Ezek 22:31; Jer 6:11). The fire consuming the foundations (yesodot, H3247) — the temple foundations themselves consumed. The destruction of the foundations represents the total demolition of what was built — not just the walls and the roof but the very foundation-stones, so that nothing can be rebuilt on the same site without first removing the rubble.
4.12: Lo he'eminu malkhei eretz vekol yoshevei tevel ki yavo tzar ve'oyev beshaarei Yerushalayim (The kings of the earth did not believe, nor did any of the inhabitants of the world, that foe or enemy could enter the gates of Jerusalem). The invincibility-assumption — not just Israel's assumption but the international consensus: that Jerusalem, YHWH's city, was inviolable. The temple mount's natural and supernatural defenses had made Jerusalem the symbol of divine protection (Ps 46:4-7; 48:12-14). The shock of the Babylonian breach is expressed as worldwide disbelief: even the kings of the earth (who were not invested in YHWH's covenant) could not believe that such a city could fall. The theological reversal: the covenant promise of protection was conditional; the unconditioned assumption of inviolability was Israel's false theology.
4.13: Mechata'ot nevieiha avonot kohaneiha hashofchim bekirbah dam tzaddiqim (Because of the sins of her prophets and the iniquities of her priests, who shed in the midst of her the blood of the righteous). The prophets and priests — the religious leadership — are assigned primary responsibility for Jerusalem's destruction. Kohanim (priests, H3548) and nevi'im (prophets, H5030): both the guardians of the covenant and the channels of divine communication have corrupted the covenant and misled the people. The shedding of righteous blood (dam tzaddiqim) recalls the prophets' indictment in Jeremiah and Ezekiel — the specific charge of judicial murder of the innocent (cf. Jer 7:6; Ezek 22:27). Covenant leadership's failure is the proximate cause of covenant community's destruction.
4.14: Na'u ivrim bachutzoim nig'alu baddam belo yukelu yig'esh billbusheihem (They wandered blind through the streets; they were so defiled with blood that no one was able to touch their garments). The priests and prophets wander ivrim (H5787, blind/blinded) — struck with the blindness that is a covenant curse (Deut 28:28-29: YHWH will strike you with madness and blindness and confusion of mind; you will grope at noon as the blind grope in darkness). Defiled with blood (nig'alu baddam) — the blood of the righteous they shed now stains their own garments, making them ritually unclean and socially untouchable. The irony: the priests who were responsible for maintaining ritual purity are now the most impure.
4.15: Suru tame qare'u lahem suru suru al tigga'u ki nazu gam na'u amru bagoyim lo yosifu lagur (Away! Unclean! people cried at them. Away! Away! Do not touch! So they became fugitives and wanderers; people said among the nations, They shall stay with us no longer). The lepers' cry (tame, H2931, unclean) — the same warning cry that the leper must make to warn others not to approach (Lev 13:45: the leper must call out 'unclean, unclean'). The priests who should have pronounced clean/unclean are now themselves the objects of the unclean-warning. Their wandering among the nations (bagoyim) and being refused further residence is the Diaspora condition of covenant curse fulfilled (Deut 28:65: among those nations you shall find no respite).
4.16: Penei YHWH chillequm lo yosif lehavitam penei kohanim lo nasa'u veziqqenim lo chananu (The LORD himself has scattered them; he will regard them no more; no honor was shown to the priests, no favor to the elders). Pene YHWH chillequm (the face of YHWH has scattered them) — YHWH's face as the dispersing agent. The loss of the divine face is the maximum theological punishment: YHWH's face turned toward Israel meant blessing (Num 6:24-26); YHWH's face turned away meant abandonment. The priests receiving no honor, the elders no favor — the social inversion: the highest honor-rank in Israelite society (priests, elders) now receives no recognition. The inversion is both political (no one respects them) and divine (YHWH no longer regards them).
4.17: Odenu techellenah eineinu el ezratenu hevel betzipiyyatenu tzippinu el goy lo yoshi'a (Our eyes failed, ever watching vainly for help; in our watching we watched for a nation that could not save). The failure of watching — waiting for Egypt's military relief that never came. The political background: Zedekiah's alliance with Egypt and the expectation that Egypt would intervene against Babylon (cf. Jer 37:5-11: Pharaoh's army that came from Egypt but then turned back). The hevel (H1892, vanity/vapor) — the same word Ecclesiastes uses for the vanity of all things: the watching for Egypt was hevel. Jeremiah had warned against trust in Egypt (Jer 37:7-9); the Lamentations poet confirms that the warning was accurate. Trust in military alliance (Egypt) as an alternative to covenant faithfulness is the typical prophetic critique.
4.18: Tzadu tza'adennu millechet birchoboteinu qarav qitzenu male yameinu ki ba qitzenu (They dogged our steps so that we could not walk in our streets; our end drew near; our days were numbered, for our end had come). Tzadu tza'adenu (they hunted our steps, H6658 + H6806) — the enemy as hunter, pursuing with such thoroughness that normal street-life was impossible. Qatzenu male (our end was full/complete) — the fullness of the appointed end had arrived. The vocabulary of 'end' (qetz, H7093) is the apocalyptic vocabulary used in Daniel (9:26-27; 11:27, 35, 45; 12:4, 9, 13) for the appointed time of completion. The siege's end is the appointed time fulfilled — not random historical accident but the arrival of the covenantal terminus.
4.19: Qallu rodefeinu min nashrei shamayim 'al he-harim dalaqunu bamidbar aru lanu (Our pursuers were swifter than the eagles of the heavens; they chased us on the mountains; they lay in wait for us in the wilderness). The eagle-speed of the pursuers — the military effectiveness of the Babylonians expressed as the swiftest of birds. The eagle (nesher, H5404) appears as a Babylonian symbol in Ezekiel 17:3 (the great eagle with great wings and long pinions, coming to Lebanon) and in Habakkuk 1:8 (their horses are swifter than leopards; their horsemen fly like an eagle). The pursuit on the mountains and ambush in the wilderness — the complete coverage of every terrain, leaving no escape route.
4.20: Ruach appeinu meshiach YHWH nilkad beshichitotam asher amarna betzillo nichyeh vagoyim (The breath of our nostrils, the anointed of the LORD, was caught in their pits, of whom we said, Under his shadow we shall live among the nations). Meshiach YHWH (H4899, the anointed of the LORD) — the technical Messianic title, here applied to the king (Zedekiah or Jehoiachin). The king as the community's collective life — ruach appeinu (the breath of our nostrils) — the one through whose life the community breathes. Nilkad beshichitotam (caught in their pits) — the military capture of the king (Jer 52:9: they captured Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho). The theological significance: the failure of the human meshiach (anointed king) to protect the community points forward to the need for a divine Messiah who will not be 'caught in their pits' but will descend into the pit and rise again (Ps 16:10: you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, nor let your Holy One see corruption).
4.21: Sisi vesmachi bat Edom yoshevet be'eretz Uz gam 'alayich ta'avor cos tishkari vetitgari (Rejoice and be glad, O daughter of Edom, you who dwell in the land of Uz! But to you also the cup shall pass; you shall become drunk and strip yourself bare). The address to Edom (bat Edom, daughter of Edom) — the hostile neighboring nation that gloated over Jerusalem's destruction (Obad 11-14; Ps 137:7: Remember, O LORD, against the Edomites the day of Jerusalem, how they said, Lay it bare, lay it bare). The bitter irony: Edom is invited to rejoice — because her turn is coming. The cup (kos, H3563) of YHWH's wrath that Jerusalem has drunk will 'pass' to Edom too. The cup-of-wrath image is the standard prophetic vocabulary for divine judgment (Jer 25:15-17; Ezek 23:31-33; Rev 16:19).
4.22: Tam avonech bat Tziyon lo yosif lehagolech chata'tech bat Edom pagad he'la al avonoteich (The punishment of your iniquity, O daughter of Zion, is complete; he will keep you in exile no longer; but your iniquity, O daughter of Edom, he will punish; he will uncover your sins). The book's closing verses offer both consolation and warning. Tam avonech (your iniquity/punishment is complete, H8552 from tamam, to be complete/finished) — the punishment has been completed; there is a limit to the judgment. The exile is not permanent. The asymmetry: Zion's punishment is complete; Edom's is just beginning. The closing verse of Lamentations is not the despair of the preceding chapters but the quiet announcement that the appointed discipline has an end — and that the hostile nations who exploited the discipline will face their own accounting.
